Each round you will clear a row of eight cards by rolling four dice. Use abilities from your training and equipment to manipulate dice and cards so you can clear each card with the exact value necessary. Multiple Alchemists with unique toolsets make each playthrough a new and engaging challenge!

—description from the designer

Weeds: The Solo Card Game is a simple solo card game that can easily be played in under 5 minutes. The goal is to finish with your garden clear of as many weeds as possible.

The Weed deck consists of 18 Weed cards and 9 Weed Killer cards. The Weed and Weed Killer cards are separated into two piles. Each Weed card is unique and has 6 images of a varying degree of weed size: Small (green) weeds with a value of 1, Medium (blue) weeds are 2, and Large (red) weeds are 3.

In Stage 1: without the use of Weed Killer you're trying to control the growth of weeds in your garden. The 18 card Weed deck is shuffled and played in order of appearance. Start by playing the top card in the middle of a clean table. Future cards are played on top of already played cards and may be turned at a desired right angle. Weed images may only cover their identical weed image (green on green, blue on blue, and red on red).

Stage 2: Once all Weed cards in Stage 1 have been played a supply of Weed Killer arrives. Using only 6 Weed Killer cards (9 are supplied) cover as many weed images as possible. The Weed Killer cards may also be turned at any right angle. Once the 6 Weed Killer cards are played most likely there will be weeds still visible. Count the number of Weed Points remaining. Hopefully you can kill all the weeds.

—description from the designer

Hellbreak is a horror-themed TCG where each player represents a fearsome monster seeking to defeat the opposing monster. Over a series of rounds, players build up resources, play multiple cards, and attack their opponent’s characters. The first player to kill the opposing monster wins!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Dragon Derby: The Sport of Wizards
description:
A fun and fast-moving family tabletop game for 2 - 10 players, ages 8+. Cheer: Huzzah! Wager! Cast Wild Magic! When the first Dragon crosses the finish line, the Wizard with the largest horde of Crowns wins.

You and other powerful wizards have gathered at the Castle Keep for the Annual Dragon Derby Festival!  Will strategy, timing, spellcasting and a wee bit of luck bring you victory? If you bet on the right Dragon you may just take home the largest coin purse and maybe even get draggin... um... bragging rights!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Age of Glory: European Warfare in the Age of Pike and Powder from the Thirty Years War to the War of the League of Augsburg
description:
This Age of Eagles expansion module is a FREE, simple, historically accurate recreation of warfare from the beginning of the 30 Years War in 1618 thru the 9 Years War at the end of the century. Yes, you can fight Breitenfeld, Nasby, Vienna and Rocroi, but the game itself concentrates on more obscure yet important battles such as Seneff, Turckheim and Saint-Denis. Such engagements are supported via clarifying existing Age of Eagles rules, modifying many more, deconflicting semantics, adding new rules and taking a LOT of decision making away from the player. Supporting this effort is a system that puts all pertinent data on the QRS or labels underneath unit and terrain stands, hopefully dropping the need to consult a 102-page rulebook all the time.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
The Associate is a worker placement game at a law firm where very little work gets done.   It's a strategy game where the goal is to bill as many hours as possible while actually accomplishing as little as possible.  It's the game partners at the nation's top law firms don't want you to play.  Its... The Associates!

The Associates is a push-your-luck worker placement game for 2-6 players.  You and your fellow players are junior lawyers at a law firm that is gearing up for a big trial for an important client.  The partners will be bringing one of you to the trial, and the associate chosen will get a huge career boost and a substantial bonus.  The catch?  Since they can't make up their minds, the partners have decided to select whichever associate bills the most hours to the case.

Turns are taken by placing meeples representing time spent at the office.  Associates will need to take on projects from the partners in order to generate billable hours for the firm.  The more active projects you have, the more hours you can bill.  However, there are other demands on your time (it is Irene's birthday after all, and that cake in the conference room is not going to eat itself) and your resources are limited.  Worse, occasionally the partners demand you actually complete something!

Can you outmaneuver the other players to be the top Associate when the date of the trial arrives?

—description from the designer

PAT4 is a fast and fun card game where players try to discard their cards by forming sets of three of the same color. The best part? You can learn how to play in about 15 seconds.

The rules are simple, but the game quickly becomes exciting as players race to complete their combinations and get rid of their cards first. It’s perfect for families, friends, and quick game nights.

PAT4 was designed to be accessible, including for colorblind players, and focuses on quick matches, interaction, and lots of laughs around the table.

—description from the designer

This expansion adds a second floor to the orphanage. When rolling dice to open doors stairs are now an option. Wooden components on stilts make up the attic.

It was an experiment mixing wooden components with the plastic and cardboard. Very limited run.

Revolutions – France 1820-1880 is an immersive political simulation for 3, 4 or 5 players.

Get ready to relive 60 years of political conflicts, civil wars and major societal choices. Whether in the Chamber of Deputies or on the barricades, Ultras, Orleanists, Bonapartists, Republicans or Socialists clash to ensure the triumph of their ideals. Each faction has its own starting situation, objectives and assets. You’ll need to constantly adapt your strategy to the situation at hand, and convince allies to impose your vision of society.

It is a very simple game in terms of military operations but an exciting journey through this epic period thanks to the richness of the events portrayed and the multi-player focus of the game.

Each turn, players begin by taking their action cards, which represent their supporters. They use them to develop their influence in six Society zones: Songs, Newspapers, Notables, Peasants, Workers and Clergy. They can also modify Social Tensions, which determine the stability of the government.

If the regime is stable, a Chamber of Deputies is elected. Players take advantage of the influence they have acquired in the Society zones to obtain votes. These votes give access to the best seats in the Chamber. Players receive political influence tokens according to their posi¬tion. Political influence is the «currency» that enables legislative action to change the current regime.

If social tensions become too great, Paris will rise up, and a civil war will begin. Players mobilize Society zones to support the government or fuel the insurrection. If the revolution triumphs, the victorious insurgents can make more radical changes to the state.

Finally, players score victory points according to the ideological objectives they have achieved.

—description from the publisher

Pomorskaya Lotsia ("Pomeranian Map") is a racing board game in which players control ships going to catch fish in the Barents Sea.

The essence of the game: each participant follows the water routes indicated on the navigation charts, catches fish, tries to avoid storms and delivers the catch to the seaports of Arkhangelsk and Onega, trying to sell it at a better price.

The playing board consists of catch, storm, and port square cards laid out in a circle. Players take turns playing navigation cards, which move their ship tokens around the cards clockwise. A player who leaves a catch card on his turn can take it as a catch. The size of the hold is limited. If a player gets caught in a storm, he must discard one catch card. The sale price in ports may vary.

•••

Поморская лоция — настольная игра-гонка, в которой игроки управляют судном, отправившимся за уловом в Баренцево море.

Суть игры: каждый участник следует водными маршрутами, указанными на картах мореходной лоции, ловит рыбу, стремится избежать шторма и доставляет улов в морские порты — Архангельск и Онегу, стараясь продать его по более выгодной цене.

Survive the storm. Get the groceries. Escape the store.

The forecast says snow.
The grocery store says panic.

In Bread & Milk, players become desperate shoppers racing to grab bread and milk before the blizzard hits. Along the way, you’ll block aisles, steal groceries, call in managers, and do whatever it takes to make it to the checkout first.

Fast, mean (in a fun way), and packed with “I can’t believe you did that” moments, this is the game that turns family night into controlled chaos.

How it works:


     Navigate crowded aisles
     Sabotage your opponents
     Collect Bread & Milk
     Check out and race to the exit
     Hope for snow


Simple. Sneaky. Surprisingly strategic.

—description from the publisher

For eleven centuries, Tistrofel drowned in endless war. Armies clashed, kingdoms burned, and the dead piled higher than the walls that failed to protect them.

Then Kao raised his tower, shattered the old world, and forced the kings to change. No more marching legions. No more fields soaked in blood. Now, the crowns meet in one place—the Arena of Kings—to decide who rules until the next tournament.

Your banner claims the land. The Arena decides who keeps it.

---

How to Play in the Arena of Kings
Battle Kings is a competitive arena game for up to five players where each of you controls a king, wields cards and dice, and leans into your king’s unique powers to win the Tournament of Kings.

1. Choose your king: Each king has a unique playstyle, from relentless tank to necromantic horror.
2. Draw, roll, and attack: Use Battle Cards, dice, and dominoes to build attacks, shields, and spells.
3. Bend the rules: Trigger your king’s abilities, spend Power Tokens, and fear the Equalizer when it appears.
4. Outlast the others: A banner lost is not a king defeated — it’s a warning. Rise again with renewed fury, rebuild your strength, and return the favor.

---

Everything you need to run the Arena of Kings straight out of the box:

Pieces box: 11 embroidered banners, 2 markers (red & green), 3 Power Crystals, 10 Power Tokens, and the Royal Seal.
Dice box: 30 dice — 5 red, 5 black, 5 blue, 5 green, 5 white, 5 grey.
Domino box: 28 mini dominoes for shields, spells, and undead minions.
Battle Card box: 54-card Battle Deck (including Jokers), 5 Turn Stage quick reference cards, 5 Path of Prestige cards, and the Equalizer.
Rulebook insert: 5 Thrones (player cards), 1 Bone Rack, and 1 rule quick reference card tucked inside.
Not pictured: A 4×6" quick reference card and 2 domino bags — one grey (King’s Guard pouch) and one black (Graveyard).
Note: Some item colors may vary between print runs (such as embroidered banners, dominoes and/or domino case, Royal Seal, and the card box).

You have been studying the dark arts of poison for many years under the tutelage of the masters of the Poisoners Guild. Now it is time for your final exam. You and your fellow initiates are led into the inner sanctum of the Guild and a cup is placed in front of each of you. In order for you to pass the exam and be admitted to the Guild . . . one of your fellow initiates can't survive!

The Poisoners Guild is a 2-4 player card game where your goal is to outsmart and outlast your fellow players in a high-stakes test of wits and nerve.  There are four deadly poisons at the table ... and you are only immune to one of them!  Plan your moves carefully - Should you check your cup?  Pour something sinister in your neighbor's cup?  Switch the cups on the table?  Or should you create a brand new deadly poison for later in the game?  And when it comes time to drink, can you still remember what is where, or who is immune to what?  At the end of the game, will you be walking out the door?

—description from the publisher

Split into two teams, each person will have a 1-minute chance to draw as many guess words as possible to rack up points for their team.

Each card with a guess word is also accompanies by 3 more 'zap' words which are mischievously similar to the guess word.  If any of them are said out loud, then the timer will issue an electric shock to the artist.  Three shocks and the round is over.

Skipped cards are discarded but count as a shock (which will be administered by the Zapping team!).  False shocks automatically score the point for the Doodling team.

Teams alternate between Doodling and Zapping until everyone has had a chance to be the artist.  The team with the most cards wins!  (The artist with the single most cards is MVD - Most Valuable Doodler)

—description from the publisher

Seeds of Empire is the seventeenth volume in Red Sash Games’ Lace Wars series of hex-and-counter war games. An EXPANSION (not a stand alone game), it continues from where the base game, Dreams of Empire, left off. Dreams of Empire was set in India during the 1730s and 1740s and featured the First Carnatic War between the British and French. Seeds of Empire covers the Second Carnatic War in the 1750s. However, both include 'sandbox' scenarios that allow the players to explore the general state of things at the time, when India was convulsed with multiple wars between powerful native kingdoms. Dreams of Empire only covered South India. Seeds of Empire adds the entirety of Central India.

The Lace Wars system is Operational in scale (that is, above the level of battles and below the level of grand strategy). Game play is similar to most other board war games, with armies marching across a gridded map, trying to stay supplied as they manoeuvre, looking for that decisive battle. In Lace Wars you are typically a theatre commander, working with whatever you have been given (usually not enough) and trying to earn Prestige for yourself, regardless of which nation actually wins the war. Dreams of Empire and Seeds of Empire are slightly different because instead of serving a single nation you are in effect a 'condottiere' serving a variety of kingdoms over the span of several years of confused conflict.

—description from the publisher

Years ago, a dark slumber fell over the world and plunged it into nightmare, twisted by a malevolent force of darkness. All seemed lost - until Hope awakened.

In this Solo (1+) rogue-light puzzle game, you navigate a high-fantasy dream world on a quest to find the Day Knight, a hero of legend, with the power to bring an end to this curse. Along the way you will meet (and unlock) many otherworldly creatures who will join you on your journey, if only briefly.

Will your travels and discoveries lead a path to the Day Knight? Or are they more myth than salvation? If anyone can, Hope will find a way.

—description from the publisher

Yeti and Sasquatch really love spending time together when they can! It can be tough for them to get together as Yeti loves the cold & snow, and Sasquatch enjoys the sun & splashing. When they do get together they really love to get a hug from their best friend!

Help Yeti and Sasquatch get hugs! Work together to move Yeti and Sasquatch next to each other on the map, and they will get a big hug! See how many hugs they can get before any player’s hand of movement cards run out.

Yeti Loves Sasquatch is a cooperative family weight logic puzzle. Programmed movement, limited communication and adorable meeples combine for a quick, fun, hug filled, experience.

Play movement cards from your hand, or use the face up movement card near the map to move Yeti or Sasquatch around the board. Land Yeti snow space and Sasquatch on a grass space next to each other and score a hug! Play until one player runs out of cards. Count up the number hearts you have collected.

—description from the publisher

In PSIQUIS, you and your group will test your intuition, creativity, and mental synchronicity in each round. The mission: to find "the perfect word" that unites two different concepts.

Main mechanics: word association, communication, mental connection
Objective: achieve the most mental matches and accumulate points
Game mode: in pairs or teams; includes 2-player mode

Earth is lost, and humanity is forced to abandon its home in search of a new world.

Across the galaxy a catastrophic phenomenon begins to unfold. Planets are being drawn toward each other, colliding and exploding in massive cosmic events. Entire star systems are disappearing.

Yet at the edge of known space scientists discover something impossible.

Two planets, Kepler-186e and Kepler-186f, are colliding but instead of exploding they remain locked together in a fragile and mysterious balance.

At their Collision Point, humanity launches an expedition to investigate.

Early probes reveal an unexpected discovery. The stability between the two worlds appears to be influenced by the native plant and animal life thriving on their surfaces. Somehow these ecosystems play a role in preventing the catastrophic collapse seen across the rest of the galaxy.

You lead a scientific team sent to explore this phenomenon. By exploring the planet, constructing research facilities, and expanding your expedition, you gather vital resources and collect plant and animal species that may hold the key to understanding why these worlds remain stable.

During the game players deploy specialists across the planet, build infrastructure, and complete missions while developing powerful combinations of technologies and research projects.

The game includes 280 unique project cards, allowing players to build their own strategic engine and shape the direction of their expedition.

However the planet itself remains unstable. A shared stability track represents the fragile balance of the environment. If the system collapses the expedition fails and all players lose the game, creating a unique strategic tension where players must decide when to contribute to planetary stability and when to rely on others to do it. This delicate balance often leads to negotiation, calculated risks, and subtle manipulation between players as everyone tries to secure their own victory without pushing the planet toward total collapse.

With hundreds of possible card combinations, evolving strategies, and strong player interaction, every expedition unfolds differently.

Every decision has a cost.

Will your expedition uncover the secrets of these worlds and secure humanity’s future
or push the fragile system toward total collapse?

—description from the designer

Game Components:

• 1 Main Game Board
• 1 Action Board
• 4 Player Boards (Spaceships)
• 4 Player Reference Cards
• 280 Project Cards
• 60 Discovery Cards
• 14 Mission Tiles
• 10 Scoring Reward Tiles
• 32 Map Discovery Tiles
• 32 Ship Upgrades 
• Resource Tokens 
• Polyomino Resource Tiles
• Astronaut Meeples 
• Animal, Plant Tokens
• Stability Marker  
• Score Markers
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Ready to hit the jackpot?
Jackpot Roll by Relatable brings the fast-paced thrill of a casino to your living room, game night, or on-the-go adventures. It's a fun and easy dice game that mixes luck, speed, and just the right amount of chaos.

Roll your dice, match your cards, and shout “Jackpot!” before anyone else does. With quick rounds, simple rules, and endless replay value, it's perfect for family gatherings, parties, or even a quick game between friends.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Twisted Caverns: A Supplement for Four Against Darkness Expanded Edition
description:
This supplement for Four Against Darkness - Expanded Edition introduces additional tiles for caverns and fungal grottoes: natural chambers and tunnels with special rules. It includes two short adventures: Truffle Kerfuffle (a fungus gathering mission) and Underground Chicken Run (a diplomatic mission among morlocks). Also included, two long treasure tables and a few new spells.

—description from the publisher

Fablepath is an expandable card game (ECG) for two or four players, who compete head-to-head in the age-old struggle between good and evil. Fablepath’s gameplay structure encourages strategy and deck-building, while incorporating story-driven elements for a fully immersive experience.

Players will play as either the Forces of Good or Forces of Evil. Players who choose to play the Forces of Good will pilot decks built around Heroes central to the narrative threads of Fablepath. Their objective is to use their Hero and 50-card deck to gain enough Plot Points to progress through each Act’s Setting. The Forces of Good player wins the game by earning enough Plot Points to progress through each of the three Acts.

The Forces of Evil’s objective is to leverage their Settings and 50-card deck to slay the Hero. Each Setting has a unique effect, triggered by the Forces of Evil player earning Plot Points. The Forces of Evil player wins the game by slaying the Hero before they can safely earn the final Plot Point of the last Act.

—description from the publisher

The Caster Chronicles is a card game where players take on the role of a “Witch”, also known as a “Caster”. Battle it out with other Witches and Casters in the πth Dimension using cards to summon Familiars and Servants, call on your Magical Caster allies to aide you, cast powerful spells, and alter the very state of the πth Dimension itself! Destroy all 7 Orbs protecting your opponent to win!

—description from the publisher

Trial of Lotus is a social deduction board game for 6-12 players, inspired by Chinese mythology. Players are secretly assigned roles as spirits of the Forest who support Jade Lotus, or shadows who follow the Black Fox.

Players play through up to 6 trials, divided into random groups via sun/moon token draws that shuffle every two trials. Each round, players secretly vote with pure or tainted hearts. the revealed results decide whether a trial is purified or tainted. The good spirits must purify the trials while the shadows aim to taint them or conceal their identity. Players may spend a Ruyi token to propose swapping participants in a trial group.

After 4 trials, the table must eliminate one player during the Cleanse Ritual before proceeding to the aftermath, 2 more trials if necessary, or the Fox's Last Strike, where the Black Fox attempts to assassinate Jade Lotus. Both factions race to win 4 trials while protecting their leaders from elimination. A flowchart is included in the rulebook.

The game features Art Nouveau illustrated character cards with 12+ unique roles, each with distinct abilities rooted in Chinese folklore and mythology.

Similar in structure to Avalon, but with randomized group mechanics and a unique 'Cleanse Ritual' that creates a climax in the game, like a day in Werewolf. Characters like Kirin, Dreamwing, and Snow Kiku give different endings to the later half of the game that creates new information dynamics.

Expand the universe of Streets of Revenge with 5 new stages. Each one introduces its own unique mechanics that enhance the gameplay experience and tactical decision-making. Additionally, you will have a roster of six new heroes to keep laying down the law across the streets of GRIDLOCK City.

In Vampires - Jeux nocturnes, each player leads a vampire clan hunting through the city over the course of a single night. A game lasts three confrontations, and the winner is the player who captures the most humans before dawn.

Each round, players secretly prepare up to two attack groups, assigning vampires and ghouls to different districts while using decoys to disguise their intentions. Districts are then revealed one by one, and conflicts are resolved in order. City effects, clan powers, vampire abilities, and ghoul captures all interact to create a tense tactical contest built on bluffing, timing, and priority management.

The core system revolves around rank resolution, hidden deployment, and Aura priority cards used to break ties and decide key effects during confrontations. Different cities and clans introduce asymmetrical powers, giving each game a distinct rhythm and style of play.

The base game includes vampire cards, ghoul cards, district tokens, human tokens, city district boards, clan boards, and Aura cards.

Expand the universe of Streets of Revenge with 4 new Sci-Fi themed stages. Each one introduces its own unique mechanics that enhance the gameplay experience and tactical decision-making. Additionally, you will have a roster of four new female characters and an arsenal of futuristic weapons to bring order to this dystopian universe. Furthermore, a new ALGNMENT CARD will enter the fray, replacing the one from the base game.

Expand the universe of Streets of Revenge with new Oriental-themed challenges. New mechanics to enhance the gameplay experience and tactical decision-making. Additionally, you will have a roster of four new characters and a selection of ninja weapons to bring order to the Chinatown district and help you advance through the qualifiers of the legendary Taikai tournament. Furthermore, a new Alignment Card will enter the fray, replacing the one from the base game.
Expand the universe of Streets of Revenge with a new stage, two new characters, and four new Final Bosses.

Esquizofrenia is an abstract strategy board game played on a hexagonal grid with 24 double-sided pieces divided into three symbol groups: circles, triangles, and stars.

Each piece shows the same symbol on both sides but in different colors. At the start of the game, four pieces are placed on the colored corner spaces of the board with the side matching the space facing down.

On their turn, a player moves one visible piece either to an adjacent empty space or by jumping over a neighboring piece into the next empty space, similar to checkers. Multiple jumps can be chained in a single turn.

When a piece jumps over another piece, the jumped piece may flip depending on whether its symbol or color matches the jumping piece. Flipping changes the color currently facing up, affecting which player may move it in future turns.

If a piece reaches a space that matches both its symbol and the color facing up, it leaves the board. Because pieces may flip during jumps, it is possible to remove more than one piece in a single turn.

The winner is the player who, on their turn, has no legal moves remaining—either because none of their pieces remain on the board or because their pieces are blocked.

Building on PLAAY Games's Soccer Blast Pro Soccer Game, Soccer Blast Express uses the same cards and concepts as the full-play version, so all previously-released card sets are entirely compatible. Familiarity with the parent game is helpful (though not entirely necessary). Soccer Blast Express has been specifically designed to capture the feel and flow of "the beautiful game" while condensing the action into a ten to fifteen minute time frame!

—description from the publisher

Koalapso is a game inspired by the classic card game "Tamalou," but completely revamped and full of surprises.

Be the koala with the fewest points to avoid the koalapso, and mess with the other players using a huge number of effects and strategies. Watch out for the pink cards, as these will force you to challenge other koalas to a battle for points.

With your cards face down, try to remember your score, and replace your cards with others that have fewer points, or use their effects to survive that round or add points to another koala.

When you think you can win or that the others are doing very badly, shout "Koalapso!" That will be the last turn for each player, and the game will end when that round is over.

Think you’ve got the best poker face at the table? Think again. That’s Spot On! cranks up the classic game of Liar’s Dice with bold twists that keep everyone guessing until the last round. Perfect for families, friend groups, and game nights of all kinds, this is not your average dice game — it’s bluffing, bidding, and strategy — and correctly yelling out “Spot On!” to help keep you in the game.

Players shake their dice and conceal them under their cups upside down on the table. Then they try to outwit each other with bold bids — guessing how many of a certain dice number are hiding under all the cups. Raise the bid, challenge a bluff, or call “Spot On!” to test your luck and your nerve. And when you don’t want to risk a bid or challenge, use your Pass or Reverse token to mix things up! Lose all your dice, and you're out. Be the last player with dice remaining to win. Bluff smart. Bid bold. Know when to use your tokens. And call out Spot On! to reclaim lost dice.

—description from the publisher

Court of Shadows is a multiplayer bluffing card game of deception, deduction, and political intrigue.

Each player secretly holds roles representing powerful figures within the Veiled Court. Players use these roles to claim abilities, manipulate coins, and eliminate rivals — but roles can be bluffed at any time.

Players may challenge suspicious claims. If a bluff is exposed, the liar loses influence; if the challenger is wrong, they suffer the penalty instead.

Through calculated bluffs, daring challenges, and careful observation, players attempt to outmaneuver their opponents and become the last player with influence in the Court.

Court of Shadows is inspired by social deduction and bluffing games such as Coup and Mafia, emphasizing quick rounds and high-tension mind games.

—description from the designer

Delving deeper into the theme, you arrive on the scene alone, but this time see a group of three civilians huddled around the corner of the street opposite the gang hideout.  Time to see if they know anything!

In this expansion, players get to approach any of the three witness a maximum of three times.

Naomi, Kurt and Doug may have seen things that can help you out.  As players draw up to three cards, you'll potentially get in-game buffs to help you take down the bad guys easier...

However, interviewing onlookers takes time and for each witness you approach, extra criminals start to enter the building. These deadly assailants come from the event deck though, making the searching of empty rooms more of a risk than ever before!

This expansion turns up the heat to 100 in this game of stealthy planning, memory recall and puzzles!

—description from the designer

Mala Leche con Plátano is an adult-themed party game designed to expose the most absurd, politically incorrect, and "dark" aspects of Chilean society. Aimed at players who appreciate unfiltered humor without censorship, the game serves as a satirical mirror of local culture, tackling everything from politics and slang to perversity and everyday stupidity. It is a standalone expansion/version of the original Mala Leche, maintaining the same irreverent spirit that aims to push boundaries and create lasting inside jokes among friends.

The gameplay follows a popular "fill-in-the-blank" mechanic. Each round, a "Dealer" presents a white card containing a phrase with one or more gaps. The other players choose the most "disturbed" or hilarious black card from their hand to complete the sentence. Once all cards are submitted, the combinations are read aloud for maximum comedic effect. The Dealer reviews all the submissions and selects the one they find the most hilarious or depraved.

The player who submitted the winning card earns "lucas" (the game's currency), and the dealer role rotates. The game continues until one player completely runs out of money. At that point, the player with the most "lucas" is declared the winner, and the player who went bankrupt must perform a "penitencia"—a forfeit or penalty—chosen by the winner

Mala Leche con Marihuana is an irreverent, adult-themed party game that expands the dark and satirical universe of the original Mala Leche. This version doubles down on exposing the most absurd, "penca," and wicked aspects of Chilean society, with a heavy emphasis on cannabis culture. Designed for players who believe humor should have no filters and no censorship, it serves as the ultimate experience for groups of friends looking to laugh at the most "incorrect" topics imaginable.

The game features a wide range of controversial themes, including politics, sex, violence, and death, while introducing specific topics like cannabis-related humor, misandry (hembrismo), and local Chilean slang. The goal is to create the most twisted and hilarious combinations to shock your friends and provide years of inside jokes.

Gameplay centers on a fill-in-the-blank mechanic. Each round, a "Dealer" plays a white card with a phrase containing gaps. The other players respond by playing their most inappropriate black card to complete the sentence. The combinations are read aloud, and the Dealer chooses the most "sick" or funny pairing. The winner of the round earns "lucas" (the game's currency).

The game continues until one player's bankroll is empty. At that point, the player with the most "lucas" is crowned the winner, while the bankrupt player must perform a "penitencia"—a penalty or forfeit—chosen by the victor.

Mala Leche con Ají is a fan-powered expansion/standalone version of Chile's most famous "fill-in-the-blank" adult party game. This edition, nicknamed the "spicy" version, was created through a democratic process involving the game’s community. Over 1,500 "terrible" phrases were submitted by fans, resulting in 80,000 votes across social media to select the 105 winning cards that represent the absolute worst and most hilarious aspects of Chilean "calaña" (low-life) culture.

The game remains true to its roots, exposing the most absurd, violent, and politically incorrect themes of society. With a focus on local ironies, politics, sex, and the "ordinariez" (vulgarity) suggested by the players themselves, it is designed for adults who believe humor should have zero filters.

The mechanics follow the classic style: a "Dealer" reads a white card with a missing phrase, and the other players play their most "out of line" black card to complete it. The Dealer then reads all the "sick" combinations aloud and awards "lucas" (the game's currency) to the player with the funniest entry.

The game concludes when a player runs out of money. The player with the highest wealth is the winner and gets to decide a "penitencia" (forfeit or penalty) for the bankrupt loser.

Our planet faces enormous economic, social, and environmental challenges. The United Nations has called upon the world to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals, a global effort to end poverty, promote prosperity and well-being for all, and protect the planet by addressing climate change.

In ODS: Jogo de Cartas, each player takes on the role of a world leader who must make crucial policy decisions to help achieve the Sustainable Development Goals.

Players must acquire Decision Cards and attach them to corresponding Issue Cards in order to resolve them and earn progress points.

On their turn, a player may perform two actions chosen from the following:
a) Draw a card from the Decisions display, possibly optimizing their choice to gain extra cards.
b) Buy a card from the Issues display.

When an Issue has three attached Decision Cards, it is considered resolved and will be worth 5 points at the end of the game.

There are also Goal Cards, which may grant additional points during or at the end of the game.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
A game of strategy and dexterity where you have to park cars and build ever-higher buildings without collapsing. Even one breath can be enough to lose the game.

Plays for 2 to 4 players, lasting 20-30 minutes. Each turn, you place your car in one of the free parking spaces, then begin building a building of your choice. Each building has a construction rule: build 2 or 3 floors at a time, or multiples.

If there are no collapses, you earn points; otherwise, you lose them. If you build a building from the ground floor or create extensions, you receive different bonuses. Whoever scores the most points wins.

—description from the designer

Un gioco di strategia e destrezza dove bisogna parcheggiare auto, elevare palazzi sempre più alti senza far crollare nulla. Basta anche solo un respiro per perdere la partita.

Gioco da 2 a 4 giocatori, durata 20/30 minuti. Ad ogni turno si posiziona una propria auto in uno dei parcheggi liberi, poi si inizia ad alzare un palazzo a scelta. Ogni palazzo ha una regola di costruzione, costruire 2 o 3 piani alla volta o multipli.

Se non ci sono crolli guadagni punti, altrimenti li perdi. Se costruisci un palazzo dal piano terra o crei degli ampliamenti riceverai bonus diversi. Chi avrà totalizzato più punti vince.

—description from the designer (Italian)

Are you spicy? Let's find out!

Draw a card, mime the clue, and see who guesses it. Add your points and see who is truly spicy!

This game also functions as a complete deck of cards, containing cards from Ace to King.

In the 9th solar month of the year 2315, the abandoned merchant vessel Antares drifted inside Saturn’s orbit, and was coupled to the Orbital Dry Dock station, Hermes.

The crew of the Hermes was unaware that 80 years before, the Antares had encountered a hostile alien life form. The alien had been brought on board when its hibernation crysalis was mistakenly collected as an ancient relic from an archaeological site on a small moon near the Alpha Centauri cluster.

Data indicates that the Antares had arrived under low power, and sensors and scans did little to confirm its condition. Once the ship was docked, and its main airlock breached, the crew of the Hermes encountered the alien life from. During the years of travel from the Alpha Centauri cluster the alien had multiplied.

External communications went dark a few minutes after initial contact. Using the collected audio and video download data from on board the facility most of the information has been pieced together.

What follows is a record of the events that occurred during the final hours on the Hermes while the crew tried desperately to lockdown the facility, and escape.

Play out the crew's final hour as they attempt to secure the facility from the alien swarm. Antares: Breach is geared for easy solitaire play, but can be expanded to up to 8 players.

Antares: Breach is a themeatic, space horror game. The actions of the crew are up to you, and the choices you make may well determine who survives, and escapes the overrun Hermes facility, and who falls victim to the alien lifeform.

Antares: Breach is the second game in the series. Telling the next chapter of the story of the doomed ship Antares, and its alien cargo.

Antares: Breach is playable solo or co-op.


        Game experience**
    



        Build** the Hermes using the Cabin and Corridor tiles. The facility must have all corridor ends connected to Cabins. Different builds of the Hermes offer different levels of difficulty.
    



        Choose** your crew selecting one side from each of the eight crewmember cards. Each crewmember has their own unique ability and choice of two starting locations.
    



        Complete** the Comms, Dry Dock, Systems, & Mainframe Access objectives. Spedning an action in the Comms, Dry Dock Ops, Systems, or Mainframe Access cabins will advance the matching counter 1 spot forward. Use of the Air Ducts can expidite movement through the facility, but the alien lifeform has found them to be useful as well.
    



        Collect** valuable Ammo and Stationary Guns from the Armory, or Medkits from Medical to kill aliens or survive an attack.
    



        Avoid** the alien lifeforms. The lifeform has multiplied diring its trip aboard the Antares, and they swarm the Hermes relentlessly. After each action performed a Scan card must be drawn which will indicate if the aliens attack, or if the crew is safe for one more round.
    



        Escape** if the crew manages to complete all objects they may regroup in the Escape Pod, and execute the Delta Launch Code to escape the Hermes Facility.
    



        Game Play**
    


Each crewmember may perform one of the following actions:


        Move** to an adjacent Cabin, Corridor or the Airducts *(Crewmembers may move into a location with an alien(s) if they have enough Ammo)*
    



        Move an Objective forward**, if a civillian is in an appropriate Cabin
    



        Collect** one Ammo or Stationary Gun from the Armory or other location if dropped/placed *(Max Ammo of 1 for some civilians/3 for Marines)*
    



        Trade** Items with another Crewmember in the same Cabin or Corridor
    



        Place** a stationary Gun in a Corridor
    



        Drop** any or all Items
    


Lay the Crewmember's matching meeple down to indicate they have taken their action for the round.

After a Crewmember performs an action, **Draw** the top card of the Scan Deck. If the Scan Card shows 4 cabins or corridors (or the Air Ducts), the crew is safe.

If it shows an image of the **alien liifeform** draw Scan cards until you draw one with 4 Cabins showing. In ascending order, place an available alien lifeform meeple in each location. If any alien lifeforms were eliminated in the previous round, move them from the **alien spawn point** card to the **Dry Dock**. If Crewmembers occupy any of these locations they must encountetr the alien lifeform.

Shuffle the deck if you exhaust the draw pile.

If there are multiple Crew in the location, the lowest numbered Crewmember is the target of the attack, and is eliminated unless they have a **Medkit** or any Crewmember in the location has **Ammo**. If there is a single Crewmember, they are automatically the target.


        Medkits** keep the Crewmember alive, but the alien lifeform remains in the location, and any Crew left must use their next action to leave the location.
    



        Ammo** Crewmembers may eliminate one alien lifeform from the current location for each ammo in their inventory.
    


If an eliminated Crewmember has items in their inventory, lay their meeple on its side to indicate the location of those items.

Once all Crewmembers have taken an action, stand the meeples up and bein the next round.

Continue taking an action with a Crewmember and drawing Scan Cards until any Crew complete the **Objectives** and **Escape**, or the alien lifeform eliminates all the crew.

Antares: Breach ships with a small, black, velvet bag for easy storage and portability.

—description from the designer

In the 3rd solar month of the year 2319, the abandoned merchant vessel Antares drifted inside Earth’s orbit, and impacted with the planet.

During the ship’s entry into the atmosphere the United Military Force tracked the incoming vessel’s trajectory to a location in the planet’s northern hemisphere.

A U.M.F. Marine tactical squad was dispatched under strict engagement protocols. There was very little information regarding the condition of the ship, or if anyone, or anything, had survived the entry, and impact with the surface. The ship had come down in a medium density habitat sector, and it was assumed there would be massive casualties.

Three fireteams were assembled and dropped into the Theatre of Operations. Their orders were to establish a sensor array perimeter around the impact site, recon all structures (including the Antares wreckage), rescue any survivors, and to eliminate any “hostiles”.

All official reports have been marked “black ops”, and there is limited intel regarding the events of the operation. This is the available event record of the fireteams’ mission to lock down the Antares crash site.

Antares: Planetfall is the third game in the series. Telling the final(?) chapter of the story of the doomed ship Antares, and its alien cargo.


        Game experience**
    



        Define** the Theatre of Operations. Lay out the Terrain cards in a grid to create the play area. A 5x5 grid works well for your first play through. A 5x5 grid will leave you with a few extra blank Terrain Cards. Suggestions for your first game are:
    


- Place the Antares Wreck in the center of your grid
- Place the 4 Sensor Array cards orthogonally around the wreck
- Place the 3 Staging Area cards along the perimeter of the grid


        Choose** your Fireteams by selecting one side from each of the 9 Marine cards. Each Marine is either a Rifleman, Medic, or Demolitions Expert, and their inventory slots will reflect this. Assign three Marines in a collumn (lowest number at the top, highest at the bottom) to each Fireteam, represented by their Insignia Card. Each Fireteam will have a Max Ammo Count basd on their inventory. You may assign Maines with Med-Kit, Burn Box, or open slots Inventory Items.
    



        Explore** the Theatre of Operations. Move your Fireteams across the Theatre grid to:
    


- Place the 4 Sensor Arrays
- Recon all structures
- Burn 9 Egg Pod Caches with Burn Box inventory items
- Rescue 9 Survivors


        Engage** the alien enemy. Each action requires you to draw a card from the Engagement Deck. The deck contains Alien Attack cards, Alien Surge cards, Fall Back cards, Egg Pod cards, Survivor cards, and extra Ammo cards. If engaging with aliens the Fireteams must spend Ammo in the amount indicated on the card. In the event a Fireteam runs out of Ammo, they lose Marines starting with the lowest numbered Marine in their column.
    



        Game Play**
    


Antares: Planetfall uses push-your-luck, and pick up and deliver mechanisms. Your fireteams have limited resources, so make your decisions carefully. If a fireteam runs out of Ammo and Medkits, they will begin taking casualties. A few rough turns can devastate your forces.

If a team runs out of Ammo, flip the Ammo card over to the **RED** side.

Each turn choose one of your three fireteams to:


        move** to an adjacent section of the map. Movement is orthogonal across the side of the grid squares, not across the points.
    



        Recon** a Structure or the Antares Wreck, and flip the map card to the Reconned Side.
    



        Place** a Sensor Array, and flip the map card to the Placed side.
    


After a fireteam's action, draw a card from the **Engagement Deck**.


        Engagement Card Types**
    



        "...all clear..."** there is no encounter.
    



        Alien Attack** the fireteam must spend Ammo equal to the strength of the alien attack.
    



        Alien Surge** ALL fireteams not in a Staging Area must spend Ammo equal to the strength of the alien surge.
    



        Ammo** the firetem may choose to take the Ammo Card to replenish their ammo, or discard it, to leave it in the Engagement Deck. Used Ammo Cards are removed from the game. Keeping these in the deck, can make later rounds easier, as other cards become hostile.
    



        Egg Cache** there are alien eggs here, and the fireteam must use a Burn Box from their inventory to destroy them. If they have no Burnbox or have destroyed 8 Cahes they must spend **Ammo** in the amount equal to the alien's strength indicated in the corner of the Engagement card.
    



        Fall back!** the fireteam must reverse its movement and spend Ammo equal to the alien strength indicated in the corner of the Engagement Card.
    



        "...incoming ship debris, take cover...** reveal the top card of the Engagement Deck and remove it from the game.
    



        shuffle** shuffle the discard and draw piles of the Engaggement Deck to form a new draw pile.
    



        Survivor** assign the survivor card to a Marine in the fireteam. They must escorting the survivor to a staging area to count them as 1 resucue. If the marine is elimnated, the survivor is as well. If there have been 8 rescues, the fireteam must spend Ammo equal to the alien's strength indicated in the corner of the Engagement card. Once a Survivor is escorted to a Staging Area place the survivor card in the discard pile of the Engagement Deck.
    



        Supplies**
    



        Ammo** fireteams are made up of three mariens each. Each marine will have a number of slots between 2 and 4 for Ammo. Ammo may be reloaded at a **Staging Area** or from an Ammo Card in the Engagement Deck. If a fireteam exhausts its Ammo, Maries are eliminated equal to the remaining alien strength. Lowest numbered Marines are eliminated first.
    



        Burnboxes** are used to destroy Egg Caches. Burnboxes must be equiped in any matching inventory slots, and may be equipped in any open slots.
    



        Medkits** keep one marine in that fireteam safe from the alien attack or surge. Medkits must be equiped in any matching inventory slots, and may be equipped in any open slots.
    



        Sensor Arrays** may be equipped in any open inventory slots. These must be placed around the Antares Wreck to form a safe perimeter.
    


If the fireteams place all 4 **Sensor Arrays**, **Recon** all structures, Eliminate 8 **Egg Caches**, and escort 8 **Survivors** to the staging areas, they have won the game.

Antares: Planetfall ships with a small, black, velvet bag for easy storage and portability.

Antares: Plantetfall is playable solo or co-op.

—description from the designer

Kemet: The Gates of Thonis is a major expansion for Kemet: Blood and Sand that introduces new maps, tiles, and a central game mechanic to deepen the strategic experience. Key Features of the Expansion:

Two New Maps:
The Renewed Battlefield (6-Player & 3-Player): An updated version of the original Kemet: Blood and Sand map for 6 players, also featuring a foldable 3-God Battle Mode for intense 3-player games.

The City of Thonis (2-6 Players): An all-new trading port map centered around an escalating game mechanic.
The Crystal Pyramid: A unique feature on the City of Thonis map. A level is added to the crystal structure each round, culminating in a dramatic final round. Controlling the Crystal Pyramid provides the ultimate strategic advantage: the ability to purchase from any colored pyramid board, regardless of a player's current position.

New Power Tiles & Miniatures:
Heliodore (Yellow) & Hematite (Gray): Two brand-new tile sets, each containing 16 unique Power Tiles plus 3 Game Up tiles and their corresponding exquisite miniatures, adding unprecedented strategic depth.

Reworked Classics: The Green tiles from the Book of the Dead expansion are being meticulously reworked and reimagined with entirely new illustrations and refreshed power.

A great battle rages..... 
And the tale will be told in the sky!

Carrion Call is a light deck building game that tells the tale of a battle between two opposing armies. The battle rages on the ground, illustrated by Carrion Call’s card play. As casualties mount the crows and vultures begin to appear in the sky above, which acts as Carrion Call’s score tracking mechanism. You must balance the damage you lay upon your opponent against managing your own casualties to keep the Flock away from your front. Over four rounds you will use a unique card matching mechanism, and powerful bonus cards to try and overpower your opponent. After four rounds the player with the least number of points over their front is the winner!

Set up

Divide the cards into the blue deck (blue outline on the face), the orange deck (orange outline on the face of the card), and the bonus deck (red outline on the face of the card). Shuffle the three decks. You will take either the blue or orange deck and give the other to your opponent. Place the bonus deck face down on the play area.

Place the board between you and your opponent with a short edge facing you and your opponent.

Place the flock tokens near the board.

Game play

You will start with a deck of 10 cards identical to your opponent's. You and your opponent both draw a random flock token to determine who plays first, higher number wins.

On your turn you will play up to 3 cards from your hand. You may play more than one card if you can make a combo from matching cards. To match cards look at the shape and number of bird silhouettes on the long edge of the card. If you have a match, you may play those cards together.

The cards come in two types. Offensive cards have black silhouettes in the top area of the card. Defensive cards have gray silhouettes in the top area. Offensive cards will allow you to place matching tokens from the flock into the flight path heading away from you (see arrows on the board). Defensive cards will allow you to move matching flock tokens from above your front into the flight path heading away from you (again, see the arrows on the board). The tokens must match the bird, or groups of birds, pictured on the card. You may not add tokens together to get the number of birds.

Take turns playing cards from your hand and moving or placing tokens, until one player has drawn their last card. This signals the end of the round. Each player get to play one more card, then the round is over.

Count up all the points from tokens over your front. Crows are face value (1, 2, or 3 points) and vultures are 5. The player with the least amount of points will draw cards from the bonus deck.

If you have the lowest total points, draw 3 cards and look at them. Bonus cards have either gray, red, or a combination of both on the face. The gray function the same as the defensive cards, move those matching tokens from over your front into the flight path. The red birds allow you to move the matching tokens from over your front over to your opponents front, skipping the flight path. Choose one of the cards to add to your deck for the next round, and pass the other two to your opponent. They choose one of the two remaining cards, and discard the third face down. You are now ready to play the next round.

Play continues for a total of 4 rounds, adding a bonus card to your deck after rounds 1, 2, and 3. At the end of the fourth round tokens are counted again and the player with the lowest total is the winner.

—description from the designer

Esquenta Eu Nunca is a Brazilian party card game that puts a twist on the classic "Never Have I Ever" format. Instead of simply asking "never have I ever..." questions, the game uses 55 unique challenge cards with dares and revealing prompts. On each turn, the active player draws a card and reads the challenge aloud. The player who drew the card must then choose someone (including themselves) to perform the challenge. If the chosen person refuses, they must take a shot of their drink as a penalty. Players earn points for completing challenges, and the player with the most points at the end wins.

Red Lantern is a 2-5 player deck and tableau building game where you lead a faction to try and take control of the city by using your agents to compete for relics, the measure of your victory.
As you add relics to your deck, you’ll lose agents, meaning as your victory grows, your control weakens.
Can you balance everything to retain control and aid the boxer uprising?

- GAMEPLAY SUMMARY -

Each round  your agents activate once as you outmaneuver rivals and expand your influence during the Box Rising.
Over five rounds, you’ll send agents to collect the most valuable relics.
Each player has the same deck of 9 agents with different values and abilities. You must decide when to make a play for the relics and when to use the agent’s abilities to to interfere with your opponent’s plans.

—description from the publisher

The Necromancer is dead! It seems he’s been dead for some time now. You, the brave villagers, must enter his tomb to clear it of his foul minions, and evil artifacts. You have only your trusted weapons to protect you, your bravery to guide you, and your fellow villagers to rely on. Clear the Tomb, save the lands from evil, and return heroes!

DarkQuest: The Card Game is a cooperative card game for 1-5 players.

Managing your hand of 3 cards, your group of Villagers must exhaust the Tomb, and clear all Minion cards from the Tomb row, before any of you take 4 wounds or run out of cards in your hands.

Choose a **Weapon Card** to use for the game. Weapon Cards list all the Minion types, and your proficiency at defeating them.

Create the Tomb Deck

The Tomb deck is made by dealing out a set number of Tomb cards.

1 Player  = 15 cards
2 Players = 25 cards
3 Players = 35 cards
4 Players = 45 cards
5 Players = 55 cards

Each player is then dealt a hand of 3 cards from the remaining Tomb cards.

On your turn you will:

Play a Card

Choose a card from your hand to play.

Minion cards: are played to the Tomb row, in the center of the play area. If three of the same Minion Icons appear in the Tomb Row, there is a Minion Surge.

Artifacts: are played on a Villager, and cause a wound. Track your wounds by sliding a Wound Tracker card under your weapon card.

Wound cards: are played on a Villager, cause a wound, and count toward a Minion Surge.

Enchantment cards: are played on a Villager, and grant a one time bonus such as healing or rolling an extra die.

Corruption cards: are played on a Villager, and that player must replace the regular white die, with the dark Corruption die.

Skull & Shield cards: Skull and Shield cards are used to buff a die roll with an extra Skull or Shield result.

You can always play Minion, Artifact, Wound, and Enchantment cards as your Play a Card action. If all Villagers are Corrupted, you may discard a Corruption card from your hand. If you have only Skull & Shield cards, or Enchantment cards that do not apply to the game state you must discard one as your Play a Card.

Minion Surge

If 3 of the same Minion Icon appear in the Tomb Row, or as a Wound card in front of a player, there is a Minion Surge. If this occurs all Villagers are Bloodied and must turn their weapon cards over to the **Bloodied** side. Being Bloodied counts toward the 4 wound loss condition. The Bloodied condition cannot be healed during the Action Phase with your die roll, and must be healed with a card. Bloodied does count toward the 4 Wounds loss condition.If a Villager is Bloodied again, mark this with the **Bloodied** side of their Wound card.

Action Phase

Roll the dice to perform 1 Action:

1. Attack a Minion: Choose a Minion Icon to attack from the Tomb Row. Looking at your weapon card determine how many Skulls you must roll to defeat that Minion. Roll the dice and compare the result to your weapon card. If the roll is successful, remove that Minion Card from the Tomb Row.

2. Heal: Look at the Heal icon(s) on your weapon card. Roll the dice and compare the result to your Heal icons. If you match the required icon(s) you may remove a wound, but not a **Bloodied** condition.

3. Search Look at the Search icon(s) on your weapon card. Roll the dice and compare the result to your Search icon(s). If you match the required icon(s) you may reveal on of the Treasure cards.

You may play Skull and Shield cards as successes to boost your roll, after rolling the dice

Treasure Cards

Treasure cards are magical protections against the Minion Surge. Each Treasure pictures one of the Minion icons it will protect against. If a **Minion Surge** occurs, and you have the matching Treasure card, remove the matching Minion cards from the Tomb Row, and remove the Treasure card from play. Wound cards stay in place, they must be healed to be removed.

Winning

If the Tomb Deck is depleted, and all Minion cards have been removed from the Tomb row to the discard pile before all Villagers have run out of cards, the Villagers win!

DarkQuest: The Card Game comes in a small velvet bag to reduce packaging costs. It has a Tag that is part of the deck, and comes unattached to the bag.

-description from designer

Did you know Elvis Presley had a middle name? Or that singer Rihanna collaborated with rapper Eminem? Show off all your music knowledge in this fun trivia game of questions and answers! With over 2,000 questions about the world of music, gather your family and friends for this musical knowledge challenge. The goal is to be the first to collect chips from all categories and reach the final stage of the board.

This is a complete game that can be played on its own or combined with other ZNE decks. Live the wild, no-limits life of Zueira Never Ends. But don't forget: if you want to be a prankster on the streets... you must follow the PUG LIFE code. A card game for people who love having fun trolling their friends. The goal is to mess with your friends and reduce their Morale Points to zero. Using cards to provoke, bluff, and prank, players eliminate each other until only the one with the most Morale to withstand all the trolling remains standing. The deck includes cards for attacking and defending, but the pranking doesn't stop there: with Challenge Cards, no one is safe — not even the quietest players can stay out of it.

Thieves’ Tails is a competitive worker-placement strategy game for 1–4 players set in the bustling city of Bramblewick, where crews of thieves compete for riches, reputation, and influence in the shadows.

Each player leads a small crew of thieves from the guild, sending them across the Slums and city to gather resources, recruit new guild members, and prepare daring heists. On a player’s turn, they place one of their crew (workers) onto a location in the slums or the city to perform an action. These actions allow players to collect valuable resources, draw Guild Member cards with unique abilities, complete jobs for coin, or prepare for future heists. Because each location has limited space, players must carefully plan their moves and anticipate the actions of their opponents.

Throughout the game, players build their crew by recruiting Guild Members, each providing special abilities that create powerful combinations and strategic advantages. Resource tokens such as Briarmarks, diamonds, scrolls, and lockpicks are used to complete objectives and trigger abilities.

The city of Bramblewick constantly changes as Day and Night event cards introduce new opportunities and complications. Guards increase patrols, city factions stir, and unexpected events reshape the strategic landscape.

After a set number of rounds, the game ends and players total their guild points earned from completed heists, recruited guild members, collected loot, and other objectives. The player with the most guild points has built the most legendary crew of thieves, wins the game and signs their name into the Thieves Tales.

Tomalle consists of four colors of numbered cards. Players must get rid of their cards by matching a card from their hand with the number on the table (playing the next or previous number). If the played card matches the color of the card on the table, the player can choose an opponent to draw more cards. In some situations, the attacked player can increase the attack and redirect it to another opponent. Despite the heavy luck factor in this style of game, Tomalle allows for some strategic decisions, such as choosing attack targets and selecting colors with fewer or more chances of triggering attacks.

Flora Funga is two games in one.

Flora: Dandelions sprout, bloom and scatter their seeds in an ever-changing cycle.  Place plants, buds, flowers and seed heads to form patterns that match your mission cards. On every turn, the landscape shifts.  Choose your missions wisely and plan your moves carefully to stay ahead.  The first person to fulfill and discard their mission cards wins the game.

Funga:  Place mushrooms to complete mission patterns, scoring points as you plant, connect and block your way to victory.  Special powers grant players unique abilities that can unexpectedly change the game. Shape the fungal landscape to score the most points and become the true master of Fungi!

Wars are won by ships and armies—but they can be lost on the docks.
— Office of Naval Intelligence briefing, 1942

NUTS! Operation Underworld is a campaign book that explores the hidden war fought far from the front lines—along the docks, warehouses, and waterfronts that kept the Allied war effort alive.

While armies clashed overseas, Naval Intelligence officers, Shore Patrols, detectives, dockworkers, and informants fought a quieter battle against espionage, sabotage, organized crime, and enemy infiltration at home.

Set in a World War II port city, this campaign places players in charge of investigating suspicious activity, managing port security, and stopping threats before they explode into disaster. Violence is dangerous, information is incomplete, and every decision carries consequences that ripple across the campaign.

This 62-page book includes:

     Background on WWII port security, Naval Intelligence, and the waterfront war
     Campaign rules for Investigations, Patrols, Arrests, and Raids
     The Active Threat system: Axis espionage vs. Organized Crime
     Port Security and Threat mechanics that evolve with player actions
     Special rules for non-lethal force, informants, and civilian interaction
     Warehouse districts, docks, ships, and urban terrain guidance
     Sample missions and a full introductory Case


The war you never saw is now on your table.

This campaign book is designed for use with the NUTS! World War II tactical rules.

"Can be found in the kitchen starting with the letter T" - Bowl... Tomato... Fork... STOP! A classic childhood game in the Singa Jogos version, recommended for up to 8 players, but can be played with as many people as you can gather.

A lively party game that guarantees great laughs! Goal: think fast, answer quickly, and earn fewer penalty points at the end of each round.

It's Halloween night, and every year on this very day, something terrifying happens at the abandoned mansion on top of the hill — the neighbors report strange shuffling sounds! It's the annual Halloween competition, held since the days of Count Dracula! Monsters and ghosts compete in the most fearsome — oops, famous — contest in the world! Supernatural, of course! Learn to use the secret passages, special powers, and abilities of each character to claim the grand trophy!

Mansão Mal Assombrada is an asymmetric chase game for 2–5 players. One player controls the Ghost team (a Big Ghost and several Little Ghosts) while the others play as classic monsters — Dracula, Frankenstein, the Mummy, and the Werewolf — each with a unique ability (Dracula passes through windows, Frankenstein captures from two spaces away, the Mummy teleports between sarcophagi, the Werewolf moves extra fast). The Ghost team can walk through walls, and the Big Ghost can swap positions with any Little Ghost as a defensive maneuver. On their turn, players spin their character's spinner and move that many spaces. Landing on the character icon lets them draw an ability card with powerful one-time effects.

The monsters win by capturing the Big Ghost, but must first eliminate all Little Ghosts. The Ghost player wins by either capturing all the monsters or collecting four Medallion pieces from treasure chests scattered across the board. Each chest collected lets the Ghost draw a card — Medallion pieces are kept, while ability cards are discarded. Monsters can also draw ability cards from chests, but this creates a strategic tension: the more cards they take, the easier it becomes for the Ghost to find Medallions.

Focus and prepare your memory for this fantastic Harry Potter board game! One player becomes the Head Monitor for a round. They will place several cards featuring characters from one of the houses and let the apprentices observe them for a short period to memorize as many cards as possible. Then, after the cards are removed, each player takes turns trying to guess where the characters were positioned, placing their cards where they remember. If correct, they earn the right to spin the wheel for points. The game progresses through all four Hogwarts houses: Gryffindor, Hufflepuff, Slytherin, and Ravenclaw. Each house has a different difficulty level, so you must stay sharp to keep up. You can use helpful spells like Revelio or Position Swap. In the end, whoever correctly identifies the most positions across the houses can become the great Head Monitor of Hogwarts and lead the other apprentices. Recommended for young wizards ages 8 and up, an instruction manual with all the rules accompanies this magical card and board game.

Think you’ll play Tend with more than 6 players? This expansion includes enough dice, coins, and cards for you to enjoy the game with up to 12 people!

You may also want to consider purchasing additional stamp markers or colored pencils (or plan on sharing).

—description from the publisher

Note: This is included in the Super Deluxe edition of the base game.
family: Players: Games with expansions that change player count

boardgame: アニマルジャンプ (Animal Jump)
description:
Flip over cards to find the animal you need and move forward! See who can reach the other side of the river faster than everyone else. But be careful—if you take too long, a hungry crocodile might catch up and eat you!

Animal Jump is a simple memory game. On your turn, flip over one animal card to try to move your piece forward. As your piece advances, the animals you need to reveal will keep changing, so try to remember where each animal card is located in order to reach the finish first.

In Excelência, each player is a unit counselor in a Pathfinders club (Desbravadores), leading young members through a year of activities.

Using a deck of 87 cards in five colors, players start with a Captain card and a hand of five cards. On their turn, a player performs one action: draw a card, invite a Pathfinder or Instructor to their unit by paying a card cost, complete a specialty by paying its color cost (Instructors in your unit reduce the cost of matching-color specialties), use their Captain's special ability, or play an action card for a one-time effect. When a Pathfinder collects three specialties, the player earns a Class card matching that color combination. Since a Pathfinder can only earn one Class, players must recruit new members to earn additional Classes.

The game ends when someone completes their second Class or the draw pile runs out (once for 2–3 players, twice for 4–5). After the trigger, one final full round is played. Players then score points from each specialty's unique condition (e.g., 2 points per blue specialty, 2 points per green person, or 2 points per compass icon), plus stars on Class cards and Campori trump cards earned mid-game. The highest score wins the Excellence Badge, with ties broken by most Classes, then largest unit.

A trivia board game themed around the Brazilian college entrance exam (vestibular).

It features over 1,400 questions covering Portuguese, Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, History, Geography, and English.

Each question comes with its solution, making it both a fun game and a study tool. Players answer questions from different subjects as they advance on the board, and the first to complete all challenges wins.

A clever family game that truly tests your vocabulary. To win, you must traverse the entire board by correctly guessing the meaning of numerous curious and unusual words, while hoping your opponents get them wrong. Each round, a player draws a card and reads a word aloud for the others to guess its true meaning. The first player to discover the correct definitions and complete the board path wins. This game develops attention and sharpens intelligence, testing the knowledge of the whole family.

During the time 970 - 931 BCE the prophet and king Solomon ruled over the United Kingdom of Israel. He was considered to be very wise, and was famously wealthy.

Many stories surrounded the great King, including the tale of the “Key of Solomon”. The key was actually a book, containing knowledge which Solomon consulted to perform magic spells using magic circles or “Pentacles”.

By drawing these magic circles, and using material components such as a sword or a ring, Solomon would invoke the power of God. Through these rituals, along with confession of sins, and prayer, he could perform these magic spells.

Use cards from your hand to complete the four Pentacles of a card, on the play surface, to collect it for its point value.

Complete special spells to steal a card from another player (skull), link your fortunes (rings), "find" the top card of the daw pile for its point value (hand), or hide your fortune from your opponents (eye).

Play to a agreed upon point total, or until all cards have been player. The different game types determine a shorter game or a long game.

—description from the designer

Cardnado is a fast-paced card game where players try to get rid of all their cards while a growing tornado of cards forms in the center of the table.

Each card played increases the chaos of the tornado and may trigger special effects that affect the next player. Players must carefully choose when and how to play their cards to avoid getting stuck.

The game continues through unpredictable turns until one player manages to play their final card and wins the game.

—description from the designer

I decided to attempt a set of rules for the American Revolution. I wanted randomized movement for a fog of war feel. 
Rules that could recreate a battle in a single evening. 
Troops to be able to attempt running away if commanded to do so.
And finally I wanted Fresh Troops troops who are stationary to fire better than other troops. 
Hopefully I have captured these concepts in this set of rules.

These rules can also be used for The French Indian War and The War of 1812. 
There are 4 AWI scenarios, 1 FIW scenario and 1 1812 scenario.

Hopefully you will enjoy these rules.

—description from the designer

Turn Sequence
1) Initiative-roll for each commander.
2) Movement- each commander moves in order of initiative
3) Artillery fire-simultaneous firing for both sides
4) Musketry fire-simultaneous firing for both sides
5) Melee-initiative winner chooses direction then fight all melees
6) Morale-check unit morale then army morale

Gone Goat is a fast-moving card game inspired by classic rummy-style play, where players build sets and runs while trying to outmaneuver their opponents. Familiar mechanics make the game easy to learn, but special action cards introduce sudden twists that can disrupt plans and shift momentum around the table.

On each turn, players draw and discard cards while working to assemble scoring combinations. Along the way, action cards allow players to interfere with opponents, steal opportunities, or change the direction of play. The result is a balance of strategy, timing, and a little mischief as players watch the table carefully and decide when to play their most disruptive moves.

The goal is to complete strong combinations of cards while managing the chaos created by other players. Rounds move quickly, and fortunes can change in a single turn as well-timed cards reshape the state of play.

A player wins by successfully building the best scoring combinations and finishing the round in a strong position while leaving opponents with fewer opportunities to complete their own sets.

The Polemos ACW Companion is the perfect partner to the main rules booklet. 
It provides the opportunity to expand the range of actions and armies outlined in the rules by providing more scenarios and army generators. 
There is also a section on extending the available action by playing campaigns, complete with a sample campaign scenario and map.

This is a comprehensive and well-researched resource that can be really useful for planning new ACW armies and projects across a range of different rules sets

boardgame: Torpedoes and Tides: A WWII Coastal Forces Wargame
description:
Torpedoes and Tides is a quick play miniature wargame about night actions involving motor torpedo boats during the Second World War. 
It puts the focus firmly on command decisions and tactics and aims at giving a fast game that mirrors the frantic and chaotic nature of coastal forces actions. 
It uses an activation mechanism that rewards risk-taking and keeps the players focussed on decision making instead of book-keeping.

This book contains all the rules needed for tactical combat, 4 generic and 4 historical scenarios, a full campaign system and 160+ unit profiles covering all major and several minor countries that took part in World War II.

Although based on the same engine seen in Galleys and Galleons and adapted to WW2 technology, this is a complete, standalone game. No other books are required.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: A dice game where two players throw one of the two dice included. Once that is done you look up the results in the high school or free style score guide to see how many points are scored. Each throw is 20 seconds, and a match is six minutes, with either 2 or 3 periods. If you throw four penalties you lose, and you win by a pin or the most points at the end of a match.

The game rules and printable elements are available for free in a pdf in the "Files" tab.

Fast Simple Real Card Hockey is a streamlined hockey simulation designed primarily for solo players who enjoy realistic statistics. Using just two standard 54-card decks (including Jokers), a printable rink sheet, and a scorecard, a full hockey game can be played in approximately 15 minutes — producing realistic goals, power plays, pulled-goalie and empty-net situations, overtime and shootouts. Track season-long statistics of simulated teams and players.

Xago is a game for two people with rules a five year old can learn, but the strategic depth to challenge the brainiest of adults.

Prove it? OK, here are the rules:


     Triangles touch triangles and blanks touch blanks.
     You get a point everywhere two of your triangles touch.
     New tiles must go on the outside.
     If a tile is surrounded on all six sides, it pops out and returns to its owner.
     The game ends when someone can’t play their turn. Whoever has the most points wins!


—description from the designer

boardgame: Pixel Palace: Building the Kingdom, One Bit at a Time
description:
Pixel Palace is a competitive strategy game for 1–5 players, designed by Joel Javan and published by Noble Twin Spires, where you take on the role of an architect in a vibrant 16-bit high-fidelity pixel art world.

Players compete to build the most prestigious palace by strategically managing resources and constructing a variety of buildings.

The game consists of a total of 110 cards, featuring 5 Foundation Cards, 30 Core Building Cards (12 Tier 1, 10 Tier 2, 8 Tier 3), 10 Expansion Building Cards (4 Tier 1, 3 Tier 2, 3 Tier 3), 60 Resource Cards, and 5 Player Reference Cards.

Game Mechanics
 ☆ Blueprint Market: Carefully select building plans from a shifting blueprint market.
 ☆ Resource Management: Balance your supply of Pixel Bricks (PIXL), Steel Beams (BEAM), and Aether Gems (AETH).
