Put your clairvoyance powers to the test and guess which Magical Item will come from the top of the card pile. Objective: Collect two cards of each magical item. There are two types of cards in the game: magical item cards and special cards. Check the identification on the front of each card.

Ravelijn is themed around a group of brave knights exploring a dangerous labyrinth to defeat mysterious enemies known as Graffers and ultimately confront a powerful Count. Each knight has unique abilities and a limited number of lives, giving them different strengths as they navigate the maze. Along the way, they uncover colored tiles that may contain bonuses, penalties, or a Graffer to battle. The atmosphere is one of adventure and competition, as the knights both cooperate in defeating threats and compete to emerge as the ultimate hero.

Gameplay is divided into two distinct phases. In the first phase, players move through the labyrinth, fighting Graffers using dice to determine whether attacks succeed. Defeating Graffers earns secret cards with numerical values, while players also try to preserve their remaining life points. Once all Graffers are defeated, players total the values of their collected cards and their remaining lives. The player with the lowest score becomes the Count for the second phase, while the others remain knights.

In the second phase, the board is flipped to reveal a battle arena where the knights confront the Count. The scores from the first phase now determine each knight’s life points, and special abilities are no longer used. Knights take turns moving and attacking the Count to earn battle tokens, while the Count moves and strikes back with stronger attacks. The game ends when either the Count defeats all knights or the knights defeat the Count.

To win the game, either the Count must eliminate all opposing knights, or the knights must work to defeat the Count. If the knights are victorious, the individual knight with the most battle tokens is declared the winner, making both survival and successful attacks crucial to achieving victory.

A 1 v 1 soccer duel. try to out guess, out think, out maneuver your opponent to work the ball down the field to take a shot on goal. First player to score three points in this fast-paced game is the winner!

Nobody wants to keep the "Cambucho"
Every time you can, you will use your cards to give the "Cambucho" to other player.

The one that keeps the "Cambucho", loses.

Milan, March 1848.
The city is rising. Barricades appear overnight. And you… command the Austrian garrison.

We present W PIUS IX — a solo wargame that places you in the role of the opposing force in one of Italy’s most celebrated uprisings.

History already knows the outcome.

Objective:
By moving Austrian Units from one adjacent area to another and fighting against Insurgent Units, the player must control (occupy) with one or more Austrian Units (excluding JGR units) the 4 Objective Areas present on the map.

The map:
The Map depicts the city of Milan in 1848. It consists of the starting area (Sforza Castle) plus 40 areas. The Game Turn track, Morale track, and AP track are also printed on the map.
The Giralda promo card from Transorient Express is a special Destination card featuring the Giralda, the iconic bell tower of Seville Cathedral in Spain.

Bloodwork is a dark fantasy co-op board game for 1–4 players, set in a world shattered by the death of its Goddess and the cosmic corruption unleashed in her wake. Lead a party of Guild Mercenaries through a branching dual‑faction campaign, choosing to side with the Xalgaran Knights or the Orcish Tribes as your decisions reshape the missions ahead. Survive perilous travel filled with ambushes, relics, hazards, and fleeting opportunities. Face tense grid‑based battles where positioning, asymmetric abilities, and risk‑reward choices define every fight.

Kepis & Bayonets: American Civil War and Franco-Prussian War supplement for Muskets and Tomahawks.

You need a copy of Muskets and Tomahawks to use this book.

Contains an overview of both periods within the book as well as army lists for both conflicts, covering the various troop types, cavalry, and artillery available. 
Also comes with period-specific traits and command abilities for the two wars, a range of generic scenarios, and also more period-specific random events. 
Comes with two campaigns that cover the American Civil War -- From Bull Run to Appomattox and Bleeding Kansas.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Captain Flip: Isla Bomba – Une bouteille à la mer Promo Card
description:
When you draw the Une bouteille à la mer (Message in a Bottle), follow these steps in order:


     Randomly take a new Treasure Card from the game box and place it next to your board.
     Return the Message in a Bottle to the game box.
     Draw and play a new tile.


Notes:


     The game is played with one extra card (or 2–3 if you are playing in the Observatory).
     Whenever you must take a Card, you must first take one of the cards in the center of the table if any are available.
     A player may have up to three Cards at the same time.

boardgame: The Victory Is Ours!: A Napoleonic Warfare Battle Manual
description:
The Victory Is Ours!: Napoleonic ruleset in Fratt’s Battle Manual series that focuses on the psychological effect of battle.

The philosophy behind the rules is the primacy of human actions as opposed to cumulative material effects of ballistic combat. In the chaos of battle, humans begin to ignore the increasing material flotsam around them and fall back on their training.

 The Victory Is Ours! portrays this reality by reducing numerous charts of material modifiers, which we think, erroneously, are the main factors in determining human behavior. Instead, we increase the role of psychology in battle as influenced by training, drill, and unit elan. The result, as years of play­ testing has shown, is a set of rules that are intuitive, not driven by copious numbers of charts, has the right amount of “historical feel,” in all the right places, and is fun. The initial challenge is to get used to the procedures for:

     Integrated Movement along the table,
     Artillery Missions, their resolution
     Proper order in carrying out Close Combat situations,


and their resolution I include mini-charts to help teach the procedures, but over time, the protocols will become second nature and intuitive, not needing the charts for reference.

The proper spirit with which to use the rules is a “kriegspiel” one. 
We are all competitive.
That is part of the fun of war gaming. 
But some of us are also playing to learn from history. 
These rules cannot possibly anticipate all situations that might occur on the table. However, when players are confronted with a new and unusual situation, remember to “untie the Gordian knot” on the principle of following the Sequence of Play, respecting the protocols for Integrated Movement, and resolving Close Actions along the direction across the table from the Operational Flank. That is the best guide. 
It is also crucial to game with a “large souled spirit” while applying common sense reasoning.

Vokabul is designed to practise making sentences in English. Gameplay is similar to scrabble. Players draw word tiles from a bag and place them on their player board. The first to make three sentences is the winner. Currently exists in two versions: English and Esperanto, each with dedicated tiles, player boards and modified rules.

boardgame: Fighting Fantasy Quest: The Warlock of Firetop Mountain – Curse of the Warlock Expansion
description:
Gamefound Exclusive! Make every game even more challenging with this boss expansion. It includes a Danger Tracker, 20 Curse Cards, and 4 new items—adding a noticeable twist to the gameplay.

The Warlock's Curse adds a level of difficulty to the game by assigning a random Curse Card to each new Danger Level (yellow, orange and red) which may turn the core mechanics on their head. From swapping your Luck and Armor values to exchanging items with the player to your left or making monsters and heroes hit harder but defend less, it forces players to think on their feet an adapt to each new circumstance, adding a new level of challenge.

The 35mm bust of Zagor can replace the Danger Track marker and reminds you of your nemesis or you can just put that on your desk and scare away pesky heroes from approaching.

boardgame: Fighting Fantasy Quest: The Warlock of Firetop Mountain – Jib-jib Cards
description:
Special extra Jib-jib card and 2 more cards that interact with it!

Jib Jib: As a Hero Action, move all enemies in currently title by 1 tile.

Was Free to those who followed the campaign before launch as well as available as an add-on.

Sunbeam Catnap is a gently competitive spatial puzzle game about forming cozy groups of same-colored cats under a constantly shifting sunbeam. Place and overlap cat cards — with one, two or three cats — to claim the warmest spots and collect cat toys. Play new cats from your hand or reposition cards already on the table to keep your cats basking in the sun. When the afternoon light starts to fade, the player with the coziest cats in the warmest parts of the sunbeam wins!

—description from the publisher

The expansion introduces several new mechanics and components to the dice-drafting puzzle:

Expanded Player Capacity: Includes everything needed to add a 5th and 6th player to the game, including new player boards and a dice tray to make the pool accessible to everyone.

Lightboards: New personal boards that offer long-term planning and strategic opportunities.

Panorama Shared Objectives: A new cooperative-style scoring mechanism where artisans must work with their immediate neighbors to score points.

Focal Point Private Objectives: Private goals that reward players for placing specific dice in designated spaces within their window.

New Tools: Includes 6 additional tool cards to manipulate dice and navigate placement restrictions.

The expansion requires the base game (or the Sagrada Refresh Edition) to play.

Who can create the highest stack of bars and score the most points until the deck of cards is empty?

Who manages to stack the highest bars and thus generate the most points? Only those with a good tactic, with a little luck, will defeat their opponent or beat their own high score.

All 63 cards are shuffled and placed in the center. In a two-person duel, one card is placed face down to the
side.

The starting player takes a card from the draw pile in the center and places this card on one of their piles in front of them, according to the rules. If they haven't yet formed a pile, they start by creating their first one.

He must always place his bar card on top of a stack with a card of a different metal type on top, thus creating a higher bar stack. A bar of the same type may never be placed on top of the card below it. If he cannot place this card on any of his existing bar stacks, he can start a new stack. Adjacent stacks must, when starting a new stack, also have a top card of a different metal type than the one in the adjacent stack. This only applies to starting a new stack; otherwise, the different metal type only applies to the existing stack being created.

—description from the designer

Choose your spaghetti wisely before you slurp it up in this topping dropping game. If your table manners are sloppy, you'll be kicked out of the restaurant!

Use tweezers to pull spaghetti strings from the tin. If removing the string causes a topping to hit the bottom of the tin, you are eliminated!

—description from the publisher

A party game of guessing sketches

The ‘artist’ describes what they are drawing using simple shapes and descriptions. Guessers draw what they hear and try to predict what is being drawn

—description from the publisher

A 1-4 player competitive board game where success depends on improvisation, creativity, and surgical precision. Travel the world, infiltrate exotic locations, and eliminate high-profile targets - but this time, you won't be alone. Other agents have also been assigned the contract, and they’ll stop at nothing to sabotage your mission and deliver the killing blow. Guns blazing or silent assassin? The choice is yours.

The game is based on the Hitman video game franchise and was developed in collaboration with IO Interactive.

—description from the publisher

Ranagedon is a tactical dexterity and area control game for 2 to 4 players, where physical chaos and precision collide in every leap. In this showdown, players command an army of frogs that must advance using a finger-pressure jumping technique, seeking to dominate the battlefield and conquer the coveted Golden Water Lily located at the center of the table.
Victory is forged through a stacking capture system: by landing on an opponent, you block them and take control of the stack, but only the disc that manages to stay on top at the end of the game can claim the points. Strategy runs deep with the use of special abilities: the Double Jump and Dive allow for long-range maneuvers, while Poisonous frogs act as a lethal defense, punishing enemy mistakes by sending them straight back to their base. Fast, tense, and highly competitive, Ranagedon challenges players to master the physics of the jump to rise as the sovereigns of the swamp.

Adrift on the open sea, a creaking raft carries a glittering pile of stolen treasure. Gems slide and tumble across the deck as pirates rock the raft to grab the richest prizes… Gather the sparkling loot and load it into your Lifeboats, and make sure to grab as much loot as possible before the Pirate Flag signals the end. When the chaos settles, the pirate who rescued the most valuable treasure wins!

—description from the publisher

A highly advanced alien AI has arrived on Earth. It can analyze data but isn't yet sure if it truly understands human speech and the complex ways we connect meanings. You have been chosen to demonstrate the best understanding of the alien AI’s team of contactors. In Contact AI, players don’t just guess words — they build, verify, and strengthen semantic connections.

Gameplay:
The game is played on a 6x6 grid of "Semantic Nodes" (word cards). Teams are led by an Interpreter who provides a clue consisting of exactly one word and one number, indicating how many nodes on the field are linked by that concept.

The Challenge:
Your goal is to be the first team to fully close your Interpretation Protocol. But the field is unpredictable:

     Anomalies: Discovering "Golden" or "Red" anomalies available to each team can grant powerful bonuses, such as extra clues or the ability to close any node on the board.
     The Black Hole: An "Interpretation Failure" (Black Hole) will immediately end your turn and force your team to skip the next one.


Key Features:

     High Replayability: A massive database of 800 carefully selected words (400 double-sided cards) and 54 different Interpretation Protocols ensure that no two games are ever the same.
     Flexible Modes:
         Team Competition: Engaging gameplay for large groups with support for 2 or 3 competing teams.
         Small Group Play: A specialized mode for 2–3 players that uses a dice-rolling mechanic to simulate an opponent's logic.
    
     No Artificial Limits: Unlike many word games, Contact AI encourages deep linguistic strategy without restrictive clue-giving constraints beyond the core rules.


Can you become the ultimate bridge between two civilizations?

—description from the designer
Designer's Notes
The genesis of Contact AI happened during 1297 days of forced silence. As a political prisoner in Belarus, I learned that the only space where one can remain truly free is within the mind and imagination. This game was my mental sanctuary.

As a mechanical engineer and a print professional with 20 years of experience, I didn’t just want to create "another word game". I wanted to build a precise instrument of communication. My goal was to reward tactical thinking and precision through the Anomaly system, ensuring that every correct guess feels like a hard-earned resource, not just a point on a scoreboard.

Every component of this prototype was designed and produced under my personal supervision to meet the highest industry standards. This is the first of many projects I hope to share with you. Thank you for helping me turn those days of isolation into a bridge for connection.

Nom, nom - the monsters went on a picknick and they are mighty hungry! But each monster only eats their favorite food. Hmmm, which monster liked the "Slimy Worm Bowl"? And which one liked "Stinky-Sock Pizza"? 
Six adorable miniature monsters sit in front of face down food pans with great anticipation. 
On your turn, you reveal a pan and choose which monster to feed it to. If the monster likes it, the pan will disappear in its belly and you get a monster coin as a reward. 
Remember which monster eats what and deduce the rest.

Add the monster action rule for more chaos and more challenge.

—description from the publisher

Four Seasons Garden is a strategic garden-management game in which players cultivate their own market garden over the course of a full year.

Plan your crops carefully: each vegetable has its own sowing season, harvest window and specific needs. Some require pollination, others must be watered regularly or protected from frost.

As the months pass, players must adapt to changing weather, pests and market opportunities. Invest in equipment such as greenhouses, beehives, irrigation systems or botanical knowledge to improve your yields.

Success depends on anticipation rather than reaction: timing crops, optimizing your garden layout and choosing the right moment to harvest and sell.

At the end of the year, the player who has earned the most money from selling their harvest wins and proves they have built the most successful market garden.

—description from the designer

The Final Challenge widens the variety of the game by adding three independent expansions in one single box. Primarily, two new epic monsters (Pit Fiend Devil and the Drider) enter the Arena, whose special attacks and abilities, such as the frightful presence, and the draw poison, will pose new fearsome challenges to the players. Furthermore, the event cards add new special rules in every game. At the very start of the game, the players must draw 4 Event cards from the pile. Every round will be influenced by one of them. Lastly, the Potion expansion introduces utterly new game mechanics. More specifically, it includes both heroes’ and monsters’ potion cards, which provide them with unique powers. They work as consumable items, and they are the only tokens that heroes can collect on tiles different from the summoning ones.

boardgame: Escape Page: The Temple of Teokiplan
description:
A stone door slams shut behind you. You came for the ancient treasure of Teokiplan… but now you’re trapped inside the temple. Strange symbols cover the walls, water twists across the floor like it’s alive, and four doors stand before you. Only one leads out. Connect the symbols, read the flow of the water, and follow the logic of the old priests to find the true exit. One mistake sends you deeper into the temple.

Escape the page! Solve logical puzzles and riddles, and find your way out before time runs out. Play solo, team up for a faster escape, or print multiple copies and race each other (competitive mode). Happy escaping!

—description from the designer

A good mime is worth more than a thousand words!

In É Proibido Falar, players take turns as the mime. The active player chooses a topic, secretly draws a letter, and must mime a word related to that topic starting with that letter. Other players throw their ball into the tower to claim the chance to guess; the first ball to reach the base has priority. Correct guesses place tokens on the board, while failures may remove them.

The game ends when a player successfully places their white token and wins immediately, or when all topics are filled, with the player who placed the most tokens winning.

boardgame: Dead by Daylight: The Board Game – Auris Box
description:
Return to The Fog with the Dead by Daylight: The Board Game Auris Box. In celebration of the 10th Anniversary of Dead by Daylight, Level 99 Games will release the Auris Box. This full storage solution holds the entire Dead by Daylight: The Board Game collection. The base game, expansions, and upgrades all fit in this all-in-one box.

This box also comes with a new Survivor, Taurie Cain, a new Killer, The Houndmaster, and their associated miniatures and components.  The box includes a new map board, and rules for variant game modes.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Truché Tango: The Ultimate Path-Building Competition
description:
The goal of Truché Tango is to move your game piece through the channels in the tiles to reach the opposite corner of the board before the other players. The game is played on a 10x10 grid of truchet tiles with each player playing on one side of the square grid as one of 4 colors: red, blue, orange, or green.

There are 2 phases to the game:

     Phase 1: Players lay down tiles to fill all 100 spaces on the game board
     Phase 2: Players move game pieces across the board through the white channels in the tiles and use Tango cards to reconfigure the board



     Each player has 30 tiles made up of 6 distinct designs (each of the 4 colors has 5 tiles of each design). All channels in the tiles will always connect to another tile's channels no matter how they are rotated or arranged. This type of design is called a truchet tile. 


GAMEPLAY:
Phase 1: Players lay down tiles to fill the board

     At each turn, a player rolls the die once to determine how many tiles they can lay on the board (1-6 moves possible per turn).
     Players can lay tiles anywhere on the board. Once a tile is placed on the board, it cannot be moved for the duration of phase 1. 
     Phase 1 lasts until the entire board is full and all 100 spaces are occupied


Phase 2: Players move game pieces across the board through a white channel and use Tango cards to reconfigure the board

     At each turn, a player rolls the die once to determine how many moves they can make (1-6 moves possible per turn) during their turn. 
     During a turn, a player can make the following moves:
         Move their game piece from one tile to another tile, or…
         Draw a tango card
    
     Players can move in any direction at any time through the channels, one tile at a time. 
     Phase 2 lasts until one player moves their game piece(s) across the board and to the opposite corner.


Tango Cards: Rules for each card below
A Tango card is a chance to reconfigure the board, but comes with some risk as not all cards help the player who pulled it.


     Rotate any tile: Player rotates any one tile on the board 
     Swap and two tiles: Player can swap any two tiles on the board
     Swap opponent’s tile: Player can swap 2 of any opponent’s tiles on the board 
     Swap your own tiles: Player can swap any 2 of its own tiles on the board 
     Place a black or white tile: Player can replace any tile on the board with a solid-colored tile (Black tiles block all paths, White tiles connect all paths)
     Opponent swaps tiles: The player whose turn is next can swap any 2 tiles on the board 
     Opponent takes two turns: Player's turn is over and the next player takes 2 turns in a row

Each player is being provided with four men of the same color. These are placed on the four circles within the balk corresponding in color to the men. All play must be from right to left round the board.

Red leads the throwing, the player on the left follows and so on round the board until a crown is thrown. The player who first throws a crown, starts playing by placing a man on the starting point. He may throw again. As soon as the other players throw a crown they may start in the same way.

Each time a crown is thrown the player is entitled to another throw. It is left to the player's own discretion, whether he moves the man already out, or if he starts out another figure.
The player moves as many spaces as thrown by the die. He must move at every throw, whether to his advantage or not, if he is not prevented.

When a crown is thrown the player moves his man on to the next space so marked, regardless of how many spaces, or of a feather, between. The player throwing a feather moves on to the next feather space, but if a crown is between, he loses the throw, for he cannot pass a crown with a feather throw

If a player throws into a space already occupied, he places his man on top of the opponent and so makes a "Double". If he makes a double the opponent must now move this double every time he throws (that is if he can move this man at all). He is compelled to move the double in preference to all his other men. The double may move off alone again when he pleases. A double cannot play into a space already occupied. If a player, carrying a double, throws two crowns in succession he may send the double back to his balk, or place himself on top of the double, thus reversing the situation. But the double must go to balk if his carrier has no other man to move.

If a double is carried past his own home color, he must play round the board again. If a double is carried into another home color he has to remain there, till he has got all his other men home first. He then plays the double down the colored spaces till he can move round the board in the ordinary way.

A space can at the same time be occupied by only 2 men. If a man carrying a double comes to a space occupied by an opponent he sends him to balk. The player cannot play into a space already occupied by one of his own men, or occupied by a double. In all such cases, if he has no other man to move, he loses the turn.

As soon as a player has got his man into the home color, he is out of danger and finally reaches home by throwing a crown.
Whoever gets his four men first on the home crown wins the game.

—user summary

Cryptogame: Le Jeu des Champignons is a family-oriented board game where players explore a forest in search of edible mushrooms while avoiding toxic ones and other obstacles that the woods can throw at them. Players move their pawns across the forest board by rolling a die, flip tiles to discover different mushrooms, and attempt to collect the best selection of safe species. Along the way they may trade mushrooms with others or face setbacks from the unpredictability of the forest.

The goal of the game is simple: be the first to gather five excellent edible mushrooms of the same species in your basket. To achieve this, players must balance memory, risk, and chance as they reveal tiles, manage their haul, and outmaneuver opponents to fill their baskets ahead of time.

After a purification ceremony at the Orchid Pavilion, Jin dynasty noble Wang Xizhi held a The Winding Stream (曲水流觞) gathering. Participants placed wine cups into a flowing stream, and wherever a cup stopped, the person there would compose a poem and drink. This elegant cultural tradition has been celebrated ever since.

The Winding Stream is a competitive puzzle game for 2–6 players with a focus on speed and strategy. Players recreate poetic patterns by arranging streams and wine cups on eight cards each round, trying to match a target layout. The player who completes the pattern in the fewest moves and shortest time earns points, testing both memory and planning skills.

This game is part of the first edition of the 9 Cards series.
publishers: 九张牌玩具公司(9 Cards Toy Company)

description:
Chaos has engulfed Malifaux.

The Governor-General is gone, undone by his attempts to become a Tyrant, and the Guild is reeling from the blow. As it struggles to keep the opportunistic Arcanists and dreaded Resurrectionists at bay, an ancient power reawakens deep within the Badlands, gathering the scattered Neverborn to her side in preparation for the coming war with the Tyrants… and then, the scouring of all human life from Malifaux.

Amidst the chaos, daring bandits take advantage of the Guild’s distraction to line their own pockets with Soulstones, Gremlins soar through the sky in a stolen airship, and the Ten Thunders turn to a power they can neither understand nor control in order to expand their holdings.

And through it all, a shrouded woman rides slowly through streets clogged with bodies, taking measure of the dead and recruiting the strongest as soldiers for the coming war…

Ripples of Fate expands the world of Malifaux, bringing seven new Masters into the battle between fate and mortal willpower. New models, new stories, and new scenarios provide a glimpse of how each Faction intends to capitalize upon the vacuum of power left behind in the wake of the Governor’s death.

The battle for control of Malifaux has begun.

No longer do you have to trust the fickle fate of a dice roll, in Malifaux you use cards, a Fate Deck, as you lead your Crew to victory with strategy, tactics and resource management, and if that isn’t enough, you can Cheat Fate.

Looking for scalability? 
Malifaux has it. Jump directly into a Scrap with 4 – 6 miniatures, ensuring a quick and brutal fight with each character having unique skills and abilities. 
Want to escalate it? 
Take it to a Brawl, bring in additional members of your faction or hire out mercenaries to do your dirty work.

—description from the publisher

There are five mini games in this box, play one or mix and match to suit your mood.

Movie Trivia - Name the horror movie based on the trivia question.

Make a Scene - The card will give you a movie, you act out a scene while other players guess the movie.

Describe It - Describe the movie on the card without using the forbidden words.

Name That Movie - Read the quote from the card, other players will guess the movie.

Shout Out - Read the descriptive phrases on the card, the first player to shout a movie that fulfills all of the requirements gets a point.

LORE: The throne sits empty, and the kingdom is fractured. Two legendary commanders, evenly matched in military might, have brought the realm to the brink of total war. Both armies boast the exact same forces—fourteen (14) of the kingdom's most elite units—but the fog of war is thick, and only ten (10) of the units join the frontlines. 
The war will be decided across three (3) critical battle zones. In each zone, commanders must secretly deploy a vanguard of three (3) units. Combat is swift and brutal; victory demands perfectly timed counter-strikes, devastating ambushes, and the assassination of enemy generals before they can execute their orders. The commander who conquers most of the three fronts will claim the crown.
However, if the battlegrounds run red and the conflict ends in a stalemate, the frontlines will collapse. The war will then be decided in a final, desperate Sudden Death clash, where the last remaining defender held in your hand must make a final stand for the throne.

INTRO & SETUP: The Strategist: Kings Duel is a competitive, 14-card micro-strategy game of deduction and prediction where two commanders clash across three battlefronts in a battle of wits. Both players command the exact same army, represented by identical 14-card decks. At the start of the duel, each player draws 10 cards to form their hand, leaving the remaining 4 cards hidden face-down in their Reserve.

GAMEPLAY: The game is played over three rounds. In each round, players simultaneously select and secretly deploy 3 cards from their hand. Once revealed, the battle begins. Card abilities resolve in a strict priority order, allowing for brutal counter-plays, assassinations, and ambushes. The player with the highest total power at the end of the round conquers that front.

VICTORY: Winning the most rounds secures the ultimate victory. However, if the players are locked in a draw after the third round, the single unplayed card remaining in your hand becomes your last line of defense to decide the victor in a Sudden Death tiebreaker. With no randomness after the initial draw, victory relies entirely on tactics - deducing your opponent's play and countering them.

—description from the designer

The Great War, World War, War to End all Wars, the only true name it has is the Last War, for now remains not enough people to fight another.
The War ended not with a bang but a cough and splutter. The disease that took hold spread far and wide. Few survived the fever and filth. With so few humans left those that dwelled in the night took up the arms of man to fight over what was left. Those scattered beings hunt the trenches of the last war for scraps, salvage and weapons.

The Last War is a game by Will Rahman-Daultrey the creator of the popular Forbidden Psalm game system. As we did with the This Quar's War game we will be bringing you a game that includes a full rulebook, tokens, dice, and plastic goodness.

—description from the publisher

The year is 1930, an era of shadows and hidden secrets. The city, once vibrant and hopeful, now whispers with sinister rumors. In dark alleys, inhabitants murmur about ancestral entities, forbidden and unnameable. The economy, like an ancient ritual, twists and contorts, fueling something beyond human comprehension.

Forgotten places emerge from the mists — decrepit houses that shouldn't exist, winding streets leading to unfathomable abysses. Dreams are haunted by visions of cosmic creatures, slithering tentacles, and eyes that see everything. Insanity spreads like a malignant fungus, corroding minds and tearing veils between our world and the beyond.

Lone investigators rummage through forbidden manuscripts, seeking unnameable truths. Fear seeps into souls like a frigid mist, and the city becomes a cosmic chessboard where ancient forces plot their return.

Objective: Find and interrupt the Ritual before the Great Old Ones are summoned. The game is played in turns, alternating between investigators, monster turns, and news from the Arkham Gazette. Players can investigate, attack, walk, guide allies, and spend items to recover sanity and hit points. Players lose if they are devoured, killed, driven insane, or if the ritual is completed and the Great Old One awakens.

Karts is a tactical race where two or more players control fantastical beings with unique abilities as they try to stay on the track. Players can hit opponents, steal crystals, and spend them to gain advantages or mess with their rivals. The action takes place across two distinct tracks filled with thematic events featuring beasts, worms, insects, and a generous helping of natural disasters.

Jogo da Rima — to have fun, just get in the groove!

In this game you will show your ability to rhyme words.

Two teams will face off in this creative rhyming game. Using cards with various words, teams will have the hourglass timer to write as many words as possible that rhyme with the drawn word — that's the Rhyme Challenge. In the Pineapple Challenge, teams take turns saying aloud a word that rhymes with the drawn word until one team can't rhyme anymore! An exciting game that can be played with 2 or more participants.

More than 550 words!

Shot, Steel & Stone is an easy-to-learn set of horse and musket era rules that first appeared in the author's best-selling book THE WARGAMING COMPENDIUM (Pen & Sword, 2013). They are suitable for playing battlegames set in the era from approximately 1685 (when bayonets became common) through to about 1850, when rifled muskets started to dominate the battlefield. Expansions to the rules are planned to cover the earlier, late Renaissance era, as well as later conflicts both in Europe and the Americas up to the 1870s.

This version of the rules has been fully updated and expanded with the collaboration of many wargamers over the course of ten years. It has been playtested in games both small and large, including huge, multi-player games in which armies numbering thousands of miniatures per side have clashed in 18th century pitched battles set in the author's Wars of the Faltenian Succession imagi-nations campaigns.

The rules are scale-agnostic, and there is no need to re-base your miniatures as long as your basing is consistent. The size of your miniatures doesn't matter either, and games have been played successfully with figures ranging from 6mm up to 30mm. It is possible to adapt to very small table sizes, or increase the number of miniatures per base for a strikingly realistic look and feel to your games.

The system is IGO-UGO, with variation to enable the non-active player to react to enemy action, and there is no need to write orders. It's entirely up to you whether you decide to remove casualties inflicted or record them using small dice, dials or other markers—even the old-fashioned method of writing them down!

A key aspect of the rules is the command and control system, which places real emphasis on the abilities of local commanders on the spot controlling individual brigades. Players must think carefully before stepping in with their commander-in-chief, lest he blunder in his attempt to take over and cause problems for the entire army.

The combat systems are fun and involve plenty of dice-rolling to deliver crashing volleys or devastating charges. One innovation in the rules is to recognise the importance of being able to strike first in melee, such as when pikemen or lancers attack units who have shorter weapons.

This edition also includes a section of more quirky and interesting rules to cover colonial warfare, including some requested by players participating in the campaigns taking place in the author's imaginary settings of Byzarbia and Dahlia-Chindrastan. Fear not, they work just as well in real colonial campaigns, such as in Egypt or India!

Games of around a dozen units per side can easily be played in an afternoon or evening, and even much larger battles rattle along at a good pace. Of course, your wargames can be played in a more leisurely fashion if so desired, with plenty of opportunities for chat as games unfold in a 'narrative' way, with the fate of favourite regiments and brigades hanging in the balance!

This version of the rules has been deliberately stripped back in what some might even consider to be an old-fashioned way, to provide the bare essentials of the rules with a simple, black-and-white presentation, in order to keep costs—and therefore the cover price—to a minimum. By doing so, the author hopes to enable as many people as possible to give the ruleset a try.

However, those who like the 'eye candy' aspect of wargaming will not be disappointed, because a full-colour, hardback 'Deluxe' edition with additional material is planned.

The rulebook contains links to downloadable PDF playsheets that you can print out at home or keep on a hand-held device, as well as additional material for inspiration.

Finally, the rules may look and play in a simple way, but they are based on the author's 50+ years experience as a wargamer with a passion for military history, and game results are always historically plausible.

—description from the publisher
family: History: War of the Austrian Succession
History: War of the Spanish Succession

description:
Pirates Dragons Treasure (PDT) Second Edition is a competitive tabletop card game for 2-6 players where you play as a pirate captain. PDT is set in a vibrant age of piracy where dragons roam, and fortunes are won and lost at sea.

Each player starts with their Captain and an empty ship, then recruits a crew, upgrades their vessel, and collects powerful armaments to improve their odds in battle against the dragon. Every turn you work to strengthen your ship, refine your strategy, and inch closer to facing off against the beast.

You are racing against the other players, competing for limited upgrades, and disrupting other captains plans with clever curses. The game includes a structured game board to track progress, health tracker dials for ship and dragon damage, and specialized decks including Plunder, Battle, and Omens that introduce variety and replayability.

To win, you must build a vessel worthy of facing the dragon, balancing speed against strength to suit your pirate style, as you race your opponents to face and defeat the dragon.

The second edition includes several significant gameplay changes, not just cosmetic updates. A few of the biggest differences are:

Omens – A new system that introduces a different world effect each round, changing how players approach each turn and adding more variability to the game.

Armaments – Players can now build a small deck of bonus attacks used during dragon battles. This fundamentally changes how the battles play and adds a new strategic layer that didn’t exist in the first edition.

Catalysts – These act as a ticking clock during the game, gradually strengthening the dragon and creating a sense of urgency that wasn’t present before, as well as augmenting the strategy players should use along the way.

There are also a number of smaller mechanical improvements and balance changes, but the three systems above significantly change how the game plays and how players make decisions throughout the game.

Overall, the scope and table presence of the game have roughly doubled compared to the first edition, and the gameplay experience is meaningfully different.

—description from the publisher

Encanados is a tile-laying game in which players act as plumbers building pipe networks. On their turn, players place one or two pipe tiles into the shared layout, connecting them logically with existing pipes. When a player completes a closed circuit, they score points based on the values of the tiles used in that loop.

The game continues until the tiles run out, with players expanding the network and trying to complete circuits before their opponents. The player with the most points wins.

Encanados has 4 game modes: Beginner, Basic, Strategic, and Solo/Cooperative. The game case comes with the Tools expansion, with two ways to integrate it into the base game, adding more strategy and infinite replayability.

Everyone holds the answer to the crime in their hands. Ask questions and discover who, where, and how the crime was committed. Whoever solves the crime first wins the game. Play with family and friends and prove you are the best detective!

Sherlock Cards is a fast investigation card game for 2–4 players, ages 6 and up, in which the objective is to discover which suspect, weapon, and location were involved in the crime at King Mansion. Players ask questions to eliminate clues and identify the three hidden cards in the center of the table.

Strike! is a competitive card and dice game in which players take the role of batters facing pitches from an automated baseball machine inside a batting cage. Each round represents a short series of swings where players attempt to predict the outcome of a pitch and time their swing accordingly.

At the start of each turn, a pitch card is revealed, representing the speed or difficulty of the incoming ball. Players then secretly choose a bat card from their hand and simultaneously select a value on a die, representing the strength or timing of their swing. The values chosen by all players are combined with the pitch value to determine the final result of the play.

Once the result is calculated, players reveal their bat cards, each of which contains two possible target values. If the final result matches one of those values, the player scores points depending on whether the match corresponds to the higher or lower number on the card. Because bat cards are limited and each can be used only once per round, players must manage their hand carefully while trying to anticipate the decisions of their opponents.

A round ends after all bat cards have been played. The player with the highest score wins the game, provided they have reached the required point threshold; otherwise, a new round is played.

Finalist of the Jalapeño Games Crumble 2025 competition

Kugelhagel – Captain is a campaign and sourcebook for the Kugelhagel wargaming rulebook, set in the Napoleonic Wars of 1813.

The book includes:

     general rules for playing Kugelhagel campaigns,
     a mini-campaign focusing on the actions of a Russian-Prussian raiding party in the French hinterland,
     extensive army lists for most of the armies involved in the Napoleonic Wars, and
     rules for solo play.


Captains is an expansion for the Kugelhagel core rulebook and can only be played with it.

—description from the publisher

Aether Wings is a fast tactical air combat game set in the alternate-history world of the Aether Continuum.

Players control individual fighter aircraft and battle for control of the sky in cinematic dogfights. Each round, pilots secretly choose maneuver and throttle cards that determine how their aircraft moves through the air. Speed, positioning, and timing are critical as players attempt to outmaneuver opponents and line up attacks.

Aircraft are represented by miniatures or tokens and move across the table using flexible movement rules rather than rigid templates. Pilots must balance aggressive maneuvers against the risk of accumulating Strain — pushing aircraft and pilot beyond safe limits.

Key Features


     Fast tactical dogfighting gameplay  
     Small squadron battles with 1–6 aircraft per side  
     Minimal bookkeeping and quick turns  
     Cinematic maneuver system driven by pilot cards  
     Miniatures-agnostic design compatible with many model scales


Games typically take 20 minutes for learning matches and 45–60 minutes for standard battles.

Aether Wings is part of the larger Aether Continuum, a connected tabletop game universe exploring air, land, and naval combat powered by mysterious Aether technology.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Solitaire 19th C. Wargames: Cold Harbor, Charge of the Light Brigade, Gunfight at OK Corral
description:
SOLITAIRE 19th C. WARGAMES contains three simulation games covering conflicts in the last half of the 19th century. It is Vol. 6 in the Minden Classics series of books published by Minden, each containing strategy wargames, specifically designed for solo play. The three games are:

ASSAULT AT COLD HARBOR (American Civil War) simulates the Union assault taking place at the Battle of Cold Harbor in Virginia on June 3, 1864. It is a solitaire design, where the player controls the Union forces, and the game system controls the Confederate forces. The object of the game is for the player to initiate a breakthrough in Kershaw’s portion of the Confederate defensive line. It uses a small hex map of the battlefield, 70 counters, with units representing Gen. Martindale’s 2nd Division of the XVIII Corps that participated in the assault, against Confederate units of Gen. Kershaw’s Division of the I Corps. The game represents about one hour of real time.

CHARGE OF THE LIGHT BRIGADE(Crimean War) allows you to replay the famous charge during the battle of Balaclava, October 25, 1854. Lord Cardigan, commander of the British Light Brigade of cavalry, has received orders to attack the Russian cannons at the far end of the valley about a mile away. Charge of the Light Brigade is a solitaire game that allows you to witness the “ride of the 600" into the Valley of Death made famous by Tennyson's poem. Small map, 30 markers,

GUNFIGHT AT O.K. CORRAL: October 26, 1881, 2nd ed. (American West) is a simple, solitaire game depicting the famous western gunfight occurring in Tombstone, Arizona Territory between lawmen (the Earp brothers and Doc Holliday), and cowboy outlaws (the McLaury and Clanton brothers). The player takes the part of the lawmen, and the game system handles the cowboys. The game simulates the "30 shots in 30 seconds" nature of the gunfight, in a rather in slow-motion, very tactical manner. Each man has an individually rated counter indicating his Gun Speed, Accuracy, Morale, and Eperience. This 2nd ed. of the game is similar to the 1st ed. but adds extra gunfighter ratings (such as Bat Masterson, Jesse James, Wild Bill Hickock) so you can create all manner of gunfight match-ups yourself. This edition also incudes several new optional rules, and record sheets. Though designed for solo play, the game may also be played by two players.

—description from the publisher

In Spell, one person tries to guess the secret word while everyone else provides clues together - but without being able to talk to each other. Taking turns, they spell out clue words intended to put you on the right track. The faster you figure out what it’s all leading to, the more points you earn as a team. A communicative whirlwind of thoughts full of “aha” moments, misunderstandings, and unexpected flashes of brilliance.

—description from the publisher

In Dicecream, you don’t just start licking at random - the dice decide on whipped cream, sprinkles, and points. With smart rolls, you combine ice-cream flavors and toppings to build the most valuable sundaes possible. The twist: you’re not limited to your own dice — you can also use the results of others. Whoever plans well and snatches the right moment gets one big step closer to the perfect creation. In the end, the winner is the one who serves up the sweetest strategic moment.

—description from the publisher

The Carpathian mountains are drowning in blood. Rome pushes north. Dacia fights with teeth and falx. And every corpse feeds something older than both empires, an ancient relic that wakes in the slaughter, chooses one vessel, and turns a single warrior into a monster that can break entire legions. Bloodmantle: Legions & Wolves is not another minis skirmish. You forge and command living formations that bleed out in plain sight. You move gods whose mirrored powers carve the sky, the war, and the night itself. Flanking is savage. Every resource you grab makes the next one more expensive. Every victory tastes like the next loss. This is deterministic, no-dice warfare where position and sacrifice are the only gods that matter

Two empires clash until one Camp burns. Or you stand together against the Bloodmantle that grows stronger with every death you cause. The same brutal core rules. No tacked-on solo mode. The more you bleed, the more the mountain changes, the more the beast learns. A heavy, punishing 90–120 minute experience built for players who hate luck deciding their fate and love when the board itself starts hunting them back. If that kind of raw, escalating war sounds like the game you’ve been waiting for… sign up.

The Mantle is already watching.

—description from the publisher

Travel back to 1930s New York City when the greatest architectural achievement of the era, the Skyscraper of Manhattan, rises from the streets. As one of four competing developers, your goal is to illuminate your vision upon this towering structure and claim your place at the top of the city skyline.

The game is played on the Empire State Building with four facades (North, South, East, West) plus a rooftop, each containing a 5x5 grid of windows. Each window exists in 3D space, connected to its mirror positions on adjacent sides and the roof. Be the first to secure more than your equal share of the building's illuminated windows to win.

On your turn, place one of your windows on any empty space that connects to your existing structure. A valid connection requires a clear line of sight, sharing at least two coordinates with another of your windows, without passing through an opponent's illuminated space. Access to the upper floors is controlled by the building's elevator, which rises one floor each round after all players have moved, gradually revealing the penthouse levels.

When a horizontal, vertical, or depth-wise sequence of five windows is completely filled, a capture triggers. The player with the most windows in that line claims all five, illuminating their entire block. If you trigger a capture you win, your placed window remains secure. Build smart, capture lines, and light up the skyline.

—description from the designer

Players each take control of a country trying to build strong portfolio of assets in the era of Covid-19. The country with the highest net worth in the end is the winner.

At the start you're assigned a random country and get some money and starting assets. During your turn you make a choice on whether to buy an asset and after that you turn over an action card from top of the deck. Action might affect only the player in turn or multiple players and might have differing effects if it's bear or bull market at the time.

Assets resemble real life companies and other things like Amazon Rainforest or Taj Mahal. They are divided in 15 sectors with some overlapping over multiple sectors. Gathering multiple assets of a sector provides a bonus in added monopoly value. Additionally, action cards may affect the value of assets in play.

The game ends after action cards are exhausted or all players but one have gone bankrupt or after a set number of rounds decided at the start of the game.

Frontier is a high-stakes, tactical card game played with the new American Playing Cards, featuring a 50-card deck divided into five distinct historical categories. The deck is split between the Confederacy, the Union, and the neutral Border States, which serve as wildcards. With each category containing cards numbered 1 through 10, players must navigate a shifting political landscape where alliances are just as important as the numbers on the cards.

The game unfolds over exactly five rounds of escalating tension, where hand capacity scales as the game progresses. Players start with just a single card in Round 1, adding one additional card each round until they reach a full 5-card hand in Round 5. This unique progression forces players to manage their resources carefully, as unused cards are kept between rounds while played or discarded cards are reshuffled into the deck, creating a dynamic environment where card counting and probability are key.

Strategy in Frontier revolves around a betting system and a rigid victory hierarchy where "Length is Supreme." During each round, players have only one chance to set an initial bet, call, raise, or fold. When the round resolves, the winner is determined by the complexity and size of their card play. High-tier plays—ranging from "5 of a Rank" to "Campaigns"—will always defeat smaller sets, regardless of the individual card values, making the pursuit of longer combinations the ultimate goal for any serious strategist.

Victory is claimed by the player with the most cash at the end of the final round. To break ties, players must master the nuances of the hierarchy, which favors exact suits and specific alliances over mixed plays or those relying on Border State wildcards. Whether you are engaging in a Tier 5 Skirmish or a Tier 1 Campaign, Frontier offers a deeply competitive experience where clever betting and tactical hand-building determine who will dominate the new American landscape.

—description from the publisher

Welcome to the Fur-nancial District, where fortunes are made and lost in a whisker.

Are you on the hunt for the next Big Fish? Ready to ride a Whale? Pump your friends into Crypto Fish? Dodge the crash? Buy the dip? Or take home those sweet, sweet, dividends?

If so, suit up and sharpen your claws.

The Market opens in 60 seconds.

Fat Cats is a stock market card game for 2-6 players where you buy, trade and crash your way through 72 million possible markets. A card game for people who love cats and making money.

—description from the publisher

Swap 'em! Snap 'em! Score 'em!

It's raining cats! But which ones have you got? And more importantly which ones have your enemies got...

Take turns trying to get rid of your cards, testing your memory and your strategic powers.

Oh! And watch out for those black cats!?

They're unlucky... for some...

Furball is a strategic card game with sabotage for 2-6 players where you test your memory, outwit your opponents and ruin their plans. A card game for people who like cats and sabotaging friends.

—description from the publisher

For 1-4 players. Dice Mania has high replay value,
a cool game mechanic, driven by 10 dice per player and 16 Dice
Mania tiles/cards.
Who will manage to generate the most points or break their own high score?
Only those who choose their dice line well and manage to form a word with it in every round can ultimately win Dice Mania.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Ports of Call: The Board Game
description:
Ports of Call: The Board Game is based on the classic computer game from 1986 created by Rolf-Dieter Klein and Martin Ulrich.

In the board game, players compete to build and expand their own shipping empires. They do so by buying, transporting, and selling containers filled with goods across the globe.

As the game progresses, players can grow their fleets, establish factories in major port cities, and produce goods for the world market.

The winner is the player who, by the end of the game, has built the most valuable shipping company.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Commander: Romans v Dacians – A Solitaire Wargame
description:
In the rugged hills and forests of ancient Dacia, Rome fights a war unlike any it has faced before. The legions advance into a land of steep ridges, hidden valleys, and fierce tribal resistance, where every skirmish can turn the tide of a campaign.

You command a small Roman force consisting of Legionaries, Praetorian Guard, Cavalry, Archers, and Spearmen, tasked with holding the line, breaking the enemy, or seizing vital ground before the Dacian horde overwhelms you.

Opposing you are the warriors of King Decebalus: swift, unpredictable, ferocious and deadly. Cavalry crash into formations with terrifying power, Warriors surge forward in wild charges, Swordsmen hold the line with grim resolve, and Archers and Falxmen harass and surprise from the rear. Each battle unfolds differently as both armies are drawn at random, events disrupt your plans, and reinforcements arrive to mix things up.

This is a fast, tense solo wargame of tactical decision making and battlefield chaos. Every turn demands adaptation. Formations matters. Every clash matters. No two battles will ever play the same, and victory is never guaranteed.

In the game, the Player will be commanding the units of the Roman Empire as they battle a seemingly endless stream of fierce Dacian fighters.

This game is a solitaire wargame. You play the game, and the enemy is controlled by the game (or ‘AI’). You will need three standard six-sided dice to play.

It is recommended that the counters provided on the final page of the game (and in the Files section here on BGG) are used to play this game (especially for the units). Simply stick them to card and cut them out. A video demonstrating how generally to do this can be found on the Mike Lambo Games YouTube channel. I find that the easiest way to make these counters is to print or copy them onto an A4 printer label, leave the ink to dry completely, then peel the back and stick to 1-2mm card. I cut them out with good quality scissors. I now also spray them with a clear lacquer spray to give them some protection and a glossier feel, but that is certainly not necessary.

The game is a traditional hex and counter wargame which is easy to learn, fairly quick to play and hard to beat.

Rules, counter sheet and 10 full colour maps are included.

—description from the designer

Fires burn atop the ruined citadel on Hellknight Hill, sending plumes of red smoke into the air that could be a call for help. Within the old keep, strange invaders from a distant land, mysterious long-lost ruins, and the machinations of a shadowy organization await discovery. Something dire is building toward an apocalyptic event, and it falls to your characters to stop the end before it begins.

This expansion includes some of the most iconic characters from the famous Age of Ashes Adventure Path for use in the Pathfinder Arena game system. You will find Nketiah, the half-elf from Ekujae's tribe as a new hero. Meanwhile, the skeletal Hellcrown, the Charau-Ka, Belmazog, and the Dragonshard Guardian will be hiding in the Arena.

Age of Ashes enhances the Core Set’s replayability by including a new hero (Nketiah) and new monsters (Skeletal Hellcrown, Charau-Ka, Belmazog, Dragonshard Golem). They all come from the Age of Ashes adventure path, the first released for Pathfinder 2nd edition. It means that this item is the best option to combine Pathfinder Arena’s unique game mechanics with the feeling coming from Paizo’s most famous sagas.

—description from the publisher

In the age before civilization, the world belongs to the beasts.

Across plains, forests, swamps, and mountains, prehistoric creatures roam freely. Rival clans send their hunters into the wilds, competing not through war but through cunning, survival, and the skill of the hunt.

In Dino Hunters, players secretly deploy hunters across the map, trying to predict where creatures will appear and where rival clans will strike. When the hunt begins, danger comes not only from the beasts themselves, but also from overcrowded hunting grounds and the shifting balance of the ecosystem.

Each round, new creatures emerge across the land, creating fresh opportunities and unexpected dangers. Some areas may be rich with prey, while others suddenly erupt with predators or massive beasts that test the bravery of even the most experienced hunters.

Players must decide where to send their hunters, when to take risks, and when to avoid crowded hunting grounds. Success earns valuable trophies that can be brought back to camp or used to complete special hunting challenges for additional rewards.

The clan that best reads the land, outmaneuvers their rivals, and survives the most dangerous hunts will earn the greatest glory.

Gameplay Overview

In Dino Hunters, rival clans compete to prove their skill by hunting the most valuable creatures roaming a dangerous prehistoric landscape. Each round, new dinosaurs appear across plains, forests, swamps, and mountains, creating shifting opportunities and risks across the map.

Players secretly decide where to send their hunters before revealing their plans at the same time. As the hunt unfolds, crowded hunting grounds, dangerous predators, and powerful beasts can quickly turn a promising expedition into disaster.

Successful hunters return to camp with valuable trophies that can be collected for points or used to complete special hunting challenges. Over the course of the game, players must read the land, anticipate their rivals, and decide when to take risks in pursuit of the most rewarding hunts.

At the end of the game, the clan with the most victory points earns the greatest glory.

—description from the designer

GAN is a fast-paced strategy card game where players compete to match the pattern of letters on a 2x2 grid before their opponent. Deceptively simple rules give way to ruthlessly challenging gameplay.

GAN is played by placing letter cards (G, A and N) on a 2x2 grid in an attempt to match the pattern on a model card. A card on the board may be moved to another square, “locking” that square until a player flips the card back to its green side. Stacks of cards may also be moved from one square to another square with a matching letter. Through these simple rules, a challenging battle of wits emerges. Players score both by matching a model card as well as for each card collected over the course of four rounds.

Stand alone, expandable, Battle-style card game, featuring a growing list of indie comics for 2 players. This box comes with 6 factions based on factions from solis game's "space lions" and "temporal odyssey". To play, both players select 3 10-car factions to shuffle together. They play characters and one-time events into their 5 available slots in hopes of damaging their rivals' deck until they run out of cards.

—description from the designer

Night is falling on the farm and we need to make sure all the animals are safe before bedtime. In Tak Tik on the Farm, our goal is to be a competent farmer, closing the animals inside the fences and making sure they are safely kept!

Be the first player to align 3 animals of your color orthogonally or diagonally, or to close a pen with 4 fences of your color. The game ends immediately when one of the victory conditions is achieved.

By purchasing a second Tak Tik of another color, it is possible to play with 3 or 4 players.

In Memoland, adventurers set out in search of the legendary hidden path said to lead to a great source of power. One player knows the correct route and must guide the others through the journey. Each wrong step sends a player back to the start — but thanks to the power of memory, they remember which spaces have already been revealed and can press forward on their next attempt.

Along the path, players collect coins, shields, and hidden lives scattered across the board. The game features three difficulty levels, offering a scalable challenge for different groups. The player who successfully traces the perfect path while accumulating the most points is crowned the Master of Memory and wins the game.

Show that you are the best builder in your region by using stones to represent the drawn words! Win the game by getting the most votes with your creations!

Inventando a Roda is a creative party game set in the Stone Age, where all players are builders in a small village competing to prove their constructions are the best. Each round, a word is randomly drawn and every builder must creatively represent it using only the stone blocks they have in hand. After building, all players vote on which creation best captures the word — and since the village is democratic, the builder who receives the most votes scores a point. The game ends after a set number of rounds, and the builder with the most points wins.

Porta dos Fundos is a party game of creativity based on the popular Brazilian comedy brand.

Each round, all players secretly complete the same open-ended phrase prompt — for example, filling in a sentence like "I can't sleep without first..." Players write whatever answer they want, and bluffing is encouraged. Once all answers are revealed, everyone tries to guess which player wrote each response. The more you mislead the group with unexpected or out-of-character answers, the better your chances of staying hidden.

The game ends after a set number of rounds, and the player who correctly identifies the most authors — or best fools the others — wins.

Show your cooking skills and mix ingredients to make the best dish! Who will be the next MasterChef? Find out in this incredible official MasterChef game!

MasterChef Brasil: O Jogo Oficial is a cooking-themed card game for 3 to 6 players published by Estrela, based on the popular TV competition. The game is played over three rounds, each divided into three phases: dish preparation, voting, and scoring.

At the start of each round, the judge deals six ingredient cards to each player. Players select four ingredients to compose their dish and discard the rest. After revealing their creations, each player briefly presents their dish and tries to convince the others it deserves the win. Then everyone votes secretly by placing tokens on the "Best Dish" and "Worst Dish" panels. The judge awards Chef's Hat tokens based on the votes received — earning more votes for best dish means more tokens, while being voted worst costs points. After three rounds, players tally their Chef's Hat tokens plus any bonus points from their dish cards. The player with the highest total becomes the new MasterChef and wins the game.

Crime Zoom is a series of investigation game for 1 and more players and for plays of about 1 hour.

Each investigation propels you into a different universe, but almost always on a crime scene.

This crime scene is spread over several cards to form a great illustration. To investigate, nothing more simple. Take a card to take a closer look. An item intrigues you? Flip the card over and find out what is this little paper on the bedside table.

An address? You can now take the card that corresponds to this track. Unless another element on the crime scene appeals you even more ?

In Crime Zoom, you will conduct your investigation freely. Discuss between players to choose the tracks to follow. And as soon as you think you have solved the case, go to the questionnaire. Who is guilty ? What is his mobile ? What is the murder weapon ? And some subsidiary questions, of course.

Do not miss the culprit otherwise you will lose the game. But if you answer the questions correctly, the number of cards you will not reveal will decide you.

The solution will answer each of the questions. The epilogue will come to tell all this in a story or a testimony history to understand the plot that has been played.

Crime Zoom: Sous le vernie is the seventh investigation of the series which is a thriller in Valence in 1991, in the world of art.

Fé Radical is a card game for families and groups that explores the reality of Christian persecution around the world. The deck contains 78 country cards, each featuring six real-world data points: global persecution rank, violence index, social and political pressure, percentage of Christians, total number of Christians, and total population.

At the start of a game, players choose one of these six categories to compare throughout the session. A first card is revealed and placed face-up to begin a timeline. On each subsequent turn, a new card is drawn showing only the country name — players must guess where it falls in the growing line from lowest to highest value. Once their guess is locked in, the card is flipped to reveal the actual data and placed in its correct position. The player who places the most cards correctly wins.

The hangout has started and the mission is simple: discover who your secret partner is, just by exchanging greetings like fist bumps, high fives, and handshakes! But beware: guessing quickly scores fewer points... so observe carefully, interact with style, and get it right at the perfect moment.

EAÊ!? is a social deduction party game for 6 to 17 players where everyone stands in a circle and tries to find their secret match through physical greetings. Each player receives a hidden card showing how they must react to three types of greetings: handshake, high five, and fist bump — either accepting or refusing each one. Somewhere in the group, another player holds an identical card, and your goal is to figure out who it is.

On your turn, you choose someone to greet with one of the three options and observe their reaction. After everyone has greeted at least once, you may use your turn to guess your match instead. Guess correctly and you claim the lowest-value scoring token available; guess wrong and you're stuck with the highest. If you're feeling confident, you can grab the EAÊ!? marker at any time to guess out of turn — but the penalty for being wrong is steep. With an odd player count, one person receives the Wildcard and tries to get falsely claimed as someone's match.

After several rounds, the player with the fewest points wins!

An epic 8-day showdown against the Crazy Pitching Octopus, who throws a barrage of balls at the annual Squid Baseball Contest!
More pitches (dice), more players (cards)
Build the strongest squid baseball team and defeat the Crazy Pitching Octopus!
Build your deck—how many cards will you flip? The cards you flip step up to bat! The remaining cards take their positions in the field to defend!
A competitive board game for 1–5 players that combines deck-building and dice rolling for a fresh new experience of luck and strategy!

Grab a partner and see if you're on the same wavelength in this brain-syncing game! Flip a card, read the prompt, and try to meld minds with your partner by blurting out the same answer, or pulling off the same action! You sync? You're safe. Miss? Flip a letter! Spell SINK and your team is out.

Did you know that the term for a group of finches is a CHARM?

The Story
Ming is a calico cat with a dream. Every morning, she slips through the cat flap into the back garden, hoping to catch herself a finch breakfast. But the finches are clever. They know Ming is coming, and they have a plan.

Their goal? Gather the whole charm  onto the old Mulberry Bush at the far end of the garden. Safe. Together. Out of Ming's reach.

Your job? Help them get there before Ming catches them all.

The Game in a Nutshell
Charm is a small solo puzzle game played on a 3x3 grid of double-sided cards. One side shows a finch. The other side shows Ming the Cat.

Each round, Ming moves onto a neighboring finch card, scattering those finches to safety.
Then you activate one finch card, using its unique ability to move finches, reclaim territory, or call friends down from the Sky.
Win by getting all the finches onto a single finch card - the Mulberry Bush.
Lose if Ming chases every finch away or takes over the whole garden.
Choose any of the 9 garden scenarios after you master the base game

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Find the fruit. Skip the spice. But don’t get stung! In this fast-paced 18-game of jungle trickery, everyone claims to be a Banana... but only one of them is telling the truth! Outsmart the pranksters, avoid the sting, and be the first to grab three bananas to win.

Stop the monkeying around. With this many lies, you’re bound to start Going Bananas!

Separate the +1 Point Banana Cards in a pile. For a 3 player game, deal out a Monkey, Banana, Chili; 4 Players: Add the Beehive; 5 Players: Add a second Banana. The Monkey reveals their card; everyone else stays secret.

Everyone except the Monkey tries to act like a yummy Banana to convince the Monkey to choose them. You can lie, argue, or make promises to get picked! The Monkey points to one person and says, "BANANA!"

If the Monkey picks a BANANA: The Monkey and the Banana BOTH take 1 Point. If they pick a CHILI: Only the Chili takes 1 Point. If they pick a BEEHIVE: The Beehive player steals a point from the Monkey if they have one. If not, they take from the middle.

Pass the monkey card to the player clockwise, shuffle the rest of the role cards, deal the role cards out, and start another round. The first player to collect 3 Points wins!

Upgi Janggi (업기장기) is a two-player variant of Janggi featuring a stacking system.
Players may stack their Chariots, Cannons, Horses, Elephants, and Soldiers into a single combined piece. However, only two pieces may be combined at a time.

A stacked piece may move in one of two ways:

It may move while remaining stacked, using the movement ability of either component piece.

Alternatively, the stack may be partially separated, allowing one of the pieces to move independently according to its normal movement.

The Janggi rule prohibiting Cannons from jumping over or capturing other Cannons remains in effect, regardless of whether the moving Cannon, the screened Cannon, or the target Cannon is stacked with another piece.

This also applies when a stacked piece containing a Cannon moves as a stack using the movement rules of the non-Cannon component.

However, if the stack is separated and only the non-Cannon piece moves, it may capture normally according to its own movement rules.

Koma Janggi (꼬마장기, コマチャンギ) is a simplified variant of Janggi played on a 7×5 board.
It is one of the commercially produced mini-Janggi variants, created by Zainichi Korean(Koreans in Japan) designer Kim Song-il.

Each side has a reduced set of pieces: Whale (King), two Flying Fish (Chariots), two Crayfish (Horses), and three Turtles (Soldiers).
The palace retains the same structure as in standard Janggi, but Advisors are replaced by additional Horses.

Players win by capturing the opponent’s Whale or by reducing the opponent to two or fewer pieces.
However, if a player is reduced to two pieces but can immediately capture the opponent’s Whale on the next move, the game continues and that player wins upon capture.

Rebecca Walks Home at Night is a single-player horror board game about finding your way home through a dark and dangerous night. Players guide Rebecca across a modular path made of cards and tiles while avoiding mysterious creatures that lurk in the shadows.

Each game presents new obstacles and encounters as the path unfolds, creating a tense and atmospheric journey. With a playtime of around 30 minutes, the game focuses on exploration, survival, and navigating the unknown until Rebecca safely reaches home.

The game features illustrated cards, map tiles, and miniature figures that help bring its horror-inspired setting to life.

Syllaboom is a fast-paced word game where players race to recall words under pressure using specific syllables and syllable counts.

Each round, a card is revealed showing four syllables that can be used to form words. At the same time, the deck displays a target number of syllables. Starting with the active player and continuing clockwise, players must quickly say a valid Indonesian word that contains one of the displayed syllables and matches the required syllable count.

Syllables may appear at the beginning, middle, or end of the word. However, each word may only be spoken once per round. As players continue naming words, the pool of possible answers shrinks and the pressure builds.

If a player hesitates too long, repeats a word already used in the round, or gives a word with the wrong syllable count, the round explodes. That player takes the card as a penalty.

The game continues with new rounds until one player collects three penalty cards and loses the game, leaving the other players as the winners.

With simple rules, quick rounds, and increasing tension as words run out, Syllaboom delivers a lively mix of vocabulary, memory, and social pressure in a compact party card game.

Farmzle is a fast-paced puzzle game where players become farmers racing to build fenced fields and capture wandering cows. Each card represents a piece of farmland with partial fences and cows inside. By arranging their cards cleverly, players try to complete enclosed pens where cows can be safely counted. Every round, new customer orders appear in the market showing the exact number of cows needed.

At the signal, all players simultaneously flip their cards to the farm side and quickly arrange them into a 2×2 grid. Cards may be rotated and repositioned as players try to create closed fences. A cow only counts if it is fully enclosed by connecting fence segments from adjacent cards. Once a player finds a configuration that captures exactly the number of cows shown on an order card, they immediately claim it before other players do.

Winning an order not only earns money but also changes the player’s set of cards. One of the farm cards used that round becomes money, while the newly won order card joins the player’s personal card pool for future rounds. This evolving card set gradually changes the puzzle possibilities for each player.

The game ends when a player collects five order cards. Players then total the money values on their order cards, and the farmer with the highest amount wins. Fast thinking, spatial reasoning, and sharp observation are the keys to building the perfect farm and capturing cows before anyone else.

—description from the publisher
