Exile is played on a hexagonal grid of hexagons (size 6 recommended) by placing and removing Go stones. White starts. Each turn, place one stone on an empty space, subject to the placement restrictions, and remove stones subject to the capturing rules.

PLACEMENT RESTRICTIONS: Stones may not be placed in “three-mutually-adjacent-stones” triangle formations. If a placement would result in the removal of the placed stone, that placement is illegal. White’s very first move must be in the corner.

DEAD REGIONS: an empty region with only “triangle-forming” placements inside it.

CAPTURING RULES: When a region becomes dead, the winner's adjacent stones are protected from removal this turn, and the loser’s adjacent stones are removed. A dead region is ‘won’ by the player with majority stones along its perimeter. If the number of perimeter stones is tied, the opponent of the player who triggered the resolution wins the tie.

CONFLICTING REGIONS: When a placement results in multiple regions becoming dead, resolutions occur in order from largest to smallest, and stones that are protected cannot be removed. If two opposing regions are tied, the opponent of the player who triggered the resolution wins the tie.

END GAME: When no legal moves remain for one player, the game is scored. When scoring, consider each region, and count the stones owned by each player along that region's perimeter. Tied regions are worth zero. Where one player has more, they earn points equal to that region's size.

—description from the designer

The task: Line up four beads.
The problem: The rules prevent you from placing the beads where you’d most like to.
The upside: Your opponent faces the same problem.
The trick: You both have to make sure the board … keeps moving!

The objective of the game is nearly the same as in the classic “GoBang” (Five-in-a-Row).

Both players take turns placing a bead on a relatively small tilting board.

A move is only considered valid if the board subsequently switches the edge it is resting on. If the board does not switch the edge it is resting on, the opponent gets a double turn.

Unlike classic GoBang, the placement rule in Goba makes it difficult to simply extend your own rows. Such an extension is only possible with tactically skillful preparation.

—description from the designer

Players lead deep-sea development companies competing for influence through resource gathering, facility construction, unit upgrades, and project completion. Over six rounds, they expand their operations, improve efficiency, and convert careful planning into victory points in a science-fiction setting of industrial underwater exploration.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Qual é a Regra? De Theo Parizzi
description:
A different challenge every round. Test your deduction skills in this exciting game! With dynamic and challenging rules, each round brings a new surprise. Decipher the other players' clues to advance on the board. The 100 different rules guarantee fun every game! Move forward and become the champion of this challenging reasoning game. Venture into this unique game and discover who will be the master of deduction!

Qual é a Regra? is a deduction party game for 3 to 6 players (ages 12+) published by Grow in partnership with influencer Theo Parizzi.

Each round, one player becomes the Master and secretly draws a card containing a hidden rule — it could involve words starting with a certain letter, specific gestures, numbers, or other patterns. The other players must figure out what the rule is by observing the Master's clues and testing their own guesses.

On your turn, you propose a word, phrase, or action, and the Master tells you whether it follows the rule or not. If your example fits, you advance one space on the board. After each attempt, you may also try to guess the actual rule. Guessing correctly early earns more points — up to ten for a first-round guess, decreasing by one each round. A wrong guess costs you points instead. The Master scores based on how many rounds pass before someone cracks the rule, so keeping it tricky pays off. Players take turns as Master, and the first player to reach the finish space on the board wins.

Odisseia Card Game is a two-player RPG-inspired card game where each player builds a deck around a champion — choosing from classes like Knight, Mage, or Barbarian — and a subclass that further shapes their strategy. Each champion starts with 25 hit points, and the goal is to reduce your opponent's champion to zero.

On your turn, you spend mana to play allies, consumables, traps, structures, mounts, scenery cards, and equipment onto the field. Combat works through three stats — attack, damage, and defense — where your attack must meet or exceed the target's defense, and actual harm dealt equals your damage value. You begin with three mana that refreshes each turn, and can convert hand cards into additional mana for bigger plays. At the end of your turn, you choose between drawing two cards or acquiring a powerful skill from your grimoire, provided your champion meets the required attribute threshold across four stats: strength, dexterity, intelligence, and arcane. When a champion drops to 10 life or below, they unlock a devastating legendary card. You can even loot equipment from defeated enemies, adding another layer of tactical depth.

A satirical, fun, and challenging party game for people with quick thinking and the ability to improvise.

Seguem Alterações do Cliente is a creative party game about the chaotic world of advertising, published by Teia de Jogos as a free print-and-play title. Two or more players take on the role of ad agencies while the rest play as demanding clients, and the goal is to be the first agency to win a set number of advertising campaigns.

Each campaign round, a product card is revealed — it could be anything from margarine to a car. The clients then secretly choose three desire cards representing bizarre requests the campaign must incorporate. The agencies have two minutes to craft a creative pitch around the product and those desires. Then the clients add a last-minute change — the dreaded client alteration — and the agencies get one more minute to somehow fit it in. Both agencies present their campaigns, the clients vote on the best one, and the winner scores a point. New agencies are formed each round based on the funniest agency name, ensuring everyone gets a chance to pitch. The first player to reach the target number of campaign wins takes the game.

Did you know that a crocodile can't stick its tongue out? Or that Michael Jackson earned over 1 billion after his death?

Você Sabia? is a trivia board game based on the popular Brazilian YouTube channel by Lucas Marques and Daniel Molo. Players move their pawns along a board from start to finish by answering curiosity-based questions spanning general knowledge, sports, cinema, and more.

On your turn, you draw a card and read a clue or statement aloud to the other players — for example, a description of a person, place, or thing. The opponents then try to guess the correct answer. If someone guesses correctly, that player earns the right to advance on the board. If nobody guesses it, the reader advances instead. Play continues clockwise with players taking turns as the reader. The first player to reach the final space on the board wins the game. A reference answer sheet is included for verification but its use is optional. Games typically last around 30 minutes, making it a quick and accessible choice for families and friends.

boardgame: Guardiões da Galáxia Vol 2: Batalha de Heróis
description:
Battle with the Guardians of the Galaxy in this challenging game! Play your cards with intelligence and strategy to win rounds and be the victor!

Each player holds a hand of hero cards numbered 1 to 12, plus tie cards, all in their chosen color. Every round, all players simultaneously select and reveal one card. The highest-numbered hero card wins the round and its owner captures all played cards. There is one twist: card 1 beats card 12 and vice versa, adding a small but important layer of bluffing. Instead of a hero card, a player may play a tie card, which has no number and forces a draw for that round. When a tie occurs, all played cards remain on the table, and the winner of the following round collects everything. The game ends once all players have played their entire hand, and whoever has captured the most cards wins.

Já! Quem? Pow! is a simultaneous card game for 4 players (scalable to 8 or more with additional copies) where each player starts with three hit points and the goal is to be the last one standing. Every player receives their own colored deck containing number cards (0–9) and five special cards: Shield, Pow, Swap Deck, Gain Life, and Swap Life.

Each round, all players draw up to three cards, then simultaneously choose one to play face-down, one to discard, and one to keep for the next round. Once everyone is ready, all chosen cards are revealed at the same time. Special cards resolve in a fixed order: Shield protects you from damage that round; Pow forces the player who played the highest number to lose a hit point instead of dealing one; Gain Life recovers one lost hit point; Swap Life exchanges all your hit points with another player; and Swap Deck trades your entire deck and hand with an opponent. If only numbers were played, the highest number chooses someone to lose a hit point — but ties cause all tied players to take damage. A chaotic mode replaces strategic hand management with blind draws from the top of the deck. The last player alive wins.

—description from the designer

The game is quite simple: a spinning top die with the words “rapa,” “put 1,” “take 1/2,” “leave,” “take 1,” and “put 2” is spun. Depending on the face that lands facing up when it stops, the player must take all the chips in the pot (“rapa”), add one chip, and so on.

Passe a Bomba is an association game that has become a consumer favorite. It is extremely fun, educational, and dynamic, perfect for playing with family or gathering friends at home, the beach, the countryside, school, or anywhere. What makes this game unique is the time bomb, an electronic device that each player passes after their turn. Players must be quick, because nobody knows when the bomb will explode!

Stardew Valley PnP is a fan-made print-and-play board game inspired by the popular farming video game, designed by Dom Leonardo. It can be played solo or cooperatively, and tasks players with managing a small farm over a series of in-game weeks, planting crops, gathering resources, and earning victory points.

Each in-game day, a weather card is drawn that may affect gameplay — rain automatically waters planted crops, while spirit conditions can add or remove dice from your pool. Players then roll six dice, and each result corresponds to a specific action: gathering wood, collecting hay, planting seeds, watering crops, fishing, or visiting the shop. Unneeded dice can be sold for gold instead. Crops require two waterings before they can be harvested for gold or held until end of the week for victory points. Fishing requires a follow-up even/odd roll to succeed. Players choose a farm layout at the start, each with a different aesthetic arrangement of planting fields. After a set number of weeks, the player with the most victory points wins.

Antarctos: Icebound Checkers is a haunting variant of traditional Russian Checkers, steeped in the cosmic horror atmosphere of H.P. Lovecraft's sonnets. The game transports players to a frozen wasteland where reality wears thin and the ice conceals ancient, slumbering terrors. But beneath this chilling veneer lies a elegantly twisted strategy game that transforms every captured piece into a tactical resource.

The core mechanic is "freezing." When a piece is captured, it is not removed from the board. Instead, it is turned upside down and becomes neutral "ice" — a frozen monument belonging to no player. Ice cannot move or capture, but any player can jump over it in any direction (forward or backward) as a springboard. Once used, the ice immediately melts and is removed from play.

Unlike standard checkers, there are no kings. The path to victory is singular and ruthless: be the first to march one of your living pieces to the opponent's home row. A piece that reaches the edge wins instantly — no promotion, no second chances. However, if a piece touches the last row but continues moving (by capturing backward), victory is delayed until it stops there.

QUICK RULES
Antarctos: Icebound Checkers

WHAT YOU NEED
A standard 8x8 checkerboard
24 pieces (12 white, 12 black)

OBJECT OF THE GAME
Be the first to bring one of your living pieces to the opponent's last row:
White — to the 8th row
Black — to the 1st row
There are no kings. A piece that reaches the edge immediately wins the game.

BASIC RULES
1. Moves
A simple piece moves only forward one square diagonally.
Moving backward is not allowed, except when capturing or jumping over a frozen piece ("ice").

2. Freezing 
When a piece is captured, it is not removed from the board. Instead, it is turned upside down. This is now a frozen piece ("ice").
Ice is neutral: it belongs to no one.
Ice disappears (is removed from the board) immediately after someone jumps over it.

3. Priority: The Living Over the Dead
If your piece can capture a living enemy piece — you must do so. No jumps over ice are allowed as long as there is a possible capture.

4. Capture
Captures can be made forward and backward.
After the first capture, the attacking piece must continue capturing as long as there are living enemies.
If ice is encountered during a capture chain, it must be jumped over (and is removed immediately).
"Turkish shot" rule: You cannot jump over the same piece (living or frozen) twice in a single turn.

5. Ice Breaking (When No Enemies Are Near)
— You may jump over frozen pieces (your own or your opponent's) forward and backward.
Jumping over a frozen piece is not mandatory unless it is part of a jump/capture chain.
— This counts as a quiet move, but the frozen piece is removed from the board.
— If another frozen piece is reachable after the jump — you must keep jumping.
— But! If after a jump you land next to a living enemy — you must capture it.

WHEN THE GAME ENDS
Breakthrough Victory — You are the first to reach the opponent's edge.
Total Freeze — Your opponent has no living pieces left.
No Moves Left — Your opponent has pieces but nowhere to move (blocked by ice or occupied squares).

IMPORTANT
A piece that reaches the last row but leaves it in the same turn (by capturing backward) does not win until it stops there.
Ice is both friend and foe. It can launch you forward — or give your opponent a springboard to your doom.

—description from the designer

Le Petit Théâtre is a cooperative game in which you must work together to stage an original play. Using Character and Verb cards, you will form sentences that will allow the actors and actresses to perform actions on stage.

Le Petit Théâtre is a unique cooperative puzzle game where grammar becomes your playing tool.
With no turn order, communicate freely to form sentences (Subject + Verb + Complement) by combining your Character cards with the available Verb cards.

Each sentence triggers a specific action on the stage grid: push a rock, pull a tree, or move away from a guard. Your goal? Achieve the objective of each scene (e.g., get the Troll to reach the Princess while avoiding the patrols).

The game is divided into 3 acts, with each booklet adding new characters, new powers, and surprising rules.

Manage your hand collectively so as not to block the staging and satisfy the final applause meter!

Civilization always advances through interaction. Two primitive tribes have each developed their own unique forms of civilization. Will the future of humanity be shaped by plunder or trade, by herding or farming? Everything begins now. Absorb your opponent's culture, manage resources, and prove that your civilization is the one history chooses.

Reverse Civilization (逆转文明) is a two-player Euro-style worker placement game. Players lead their tribes by sending members to collect resources, exchange cultural knowledge, and establish their civilization’s dominance. Each turn unlocks unique action spaces that can later be used by the opponent after a “Reverse” card is played. Players must strategically place workers and leverage action space effects to gain monuments. The first player to place all five of their civilization monuments or the player with the most monuments when the game ends wins.

This game is part of the first edition of the 9 Cards series.

Gameplay overview:

     Each turn consists of Harvest, Progress, Action, and Cleanup phases.
     Workers are placed on yellow (production), blue (functional), or red (interactive) spaces to gather resources or trigger special effects.
     Players spend resources to place their Civilization Monuments. The first to place five monuments wins immediately.
     Cards with a “Reverse” icon are rotated 180° during cleanup, allowing opponents to use the other side.
     Game ends either when a player places five monuments or the deck runs out, with tie-breakers based on total resources and positioning.
publishers: 九张牌玩具公司(9 Cards Toy Company)

description:
American troops landed on Japanese-occupied Mindanao in April 1945, beginning a month-long campaign that saw heavy fighting in the eastern half of the island before organized resistance broke down. Scattered Japanese groups kept fighting until the Empire surrendered in August 1945.

Mindanao 1945 is a Campaign Study based on these intense battles to liberate the southernmost large islands of the Philippines. We tell the story of this bitter but little-known fight, and you get 11 new Panzer Grenadier scenarios so you can play along with the history.

Mindanao 1945 is an infantry fight, in very rough terrain. The Americans deployed the veteran 24th Infantry Division, by this point nearly burned out from constant campaigning, and the Alabama National Guard’s 31st “Dixie” Infantry Division. The Japanese defended Mindanao with the 100th Infantry Division, a decidedly second-line occupation unit, and the remainder of the 30th “Panther” Infantry Division, most of which had been destroyed on Leyte. You’ll need Saipan 1944, Pacific Armor, Leyte 1944, Dragon’s Teeth, and Marianas 1944 to play the scenarios.

—description from the publisher

Il Trono di Carte ("The Throne of Cards") is a light historical card-driven game set during the Italian Risorgimento. It is a two-player game in which players take on the roles of Victor Emmanuel II, King of Italy, and Francis II of Bourbon, King of the Two Sicilies. The game begins after Garibaldi’s victorious Expedition of the Thousand, when the Italian state has just been unified. After his defeat, Francis II takes refuge with Pope Pius IX in Rome and attempts to reclaim his kingdom by any means at his disposal—diplomacy, intrigue, propaganda, guerrilla warfare... His last cards to play are adventurers, bandits, criminal patriots... in a word: Brigands! The game takes place during the period of the great war for Southern Italy, the War on Brigandage.
 A five-year period (1861–1865) that saw tens of thousands of soldiers pitted against thousands of outlaws in Italy’s first civil war, one that was never officially declared.
  Players compete in both the political and military spheres, using cards that simulate the various available assets. Careful use of one’s resources and the pursuit of strictly historical card combinations allow players to recreate this conflict that bloodied the unification of the Kingdom of Italy. The game was produced under the patronage of the National Museum of the Risorgimento in Turin, Italy.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Komplotix is a wild party card game where you have to piece together absurd conspiracy theories involving Reptilians, Area 51, and Britney Spears fans. 
But watch out: your friends can sabotage you or steal from you using any ACTION cards!

Gameplay:
A complete theory consists of 4 cards—AGENT, OBJECTIVE, TARGET, and CONTEXT—played in that specific order. 
Playing a theory earns you 1 point, and an additional point can be gained during the voting phases. 
The first player to reach the predetermined target score claims victory!

Easy to pick up and play, KOMPLOTIX is an absolute must-have for any successful party.

—description from the publisher

Earth, 2562. Following a cataclysmic event, time and space are ripping apart at the seams.

Players must help save the world by rediscovering ancient technologies, enlisting the help of robotic time-travelling, portal-building animals known as CHRONOCRITTERS. The availability of the different actions and resources will change and flow with the passing of time. Players will gain access to more locations via time portals, but they only remain stable for a short time and will begin to decay, requiring players to constantly maintain them or build anew.

The player who can best master the chronocritters, and persuade the AI about their abilities, will restore reality, and win the game.

—description from the publisher

Double Cross is a game for 2 to 3 players, played with the eight-sided die (for 2 players) and with the twelve-sided die (for 3 players).

The players play:

6 times three identical symbols

4 times four identical symbols

4 times five identical symbols

All game boards are played at the same time and all players use the same sheet of paper.

Players take turns rolling the die.

The players first decide who is X, O, and –.

If the player who is X rolls an X, he may place his X randomly in one of the spaces.

If this player rolls an O or –, then he must fill in an O or – somewhere.

If a player rolls XX, OO, or --, then he must place the symbol twice.

If the game is played with three players (using the twelve-sided die), then when someone rolls XX, OO, or -- they must place these symbols next to each other in the same square.

This rule does not apply when the game is played with two players (using the eight-sided die).

The goal of the game is to get 3, 4, or 5 identical symbols in a row.

On a game board where 3, 4, or 5 in a row has been achieved, no more symbols may be filled in.

On a game board where it is no longer possible to score 3, 4, or 5 identical symbols in a row, nothing else may be filled in either.

When all game boards have been played, the points are added up.

The player with the highest number of points is the winner.

Winner of a 3-in-a-row game gets 3 points.
Winner of a 4-in-a-row game gets 4 points.
Winner of a 5-in-a-row game gets 5 points.
publishers: Van der Lee, International Trading B.V.

description:
Korpi is a cooperation board game based on Finnish folklore, mythology and history. The game also features a mobile application and NFC connection to bring in some magic from outside the box. 
Game has multiple stories which are played in two parts, starting from adventuring phase and ending in battle phase. During the adventuring phase players take turns to explore the adventure board and encounter various events with multiple choices for the player. The events reward players with artefacts, penalties and bonuses to nature connection and to player health points. The story ends in the battle phase where the players encounter the villain of the story and fight them. During the fight the accumulated nature connection will have an effect and the artefacts found may play a crucial role for the players.
Player's emerge victorius when they defeat the villain in the battle phase. Players are defeated if all players fall in the battle against the villain.
A hilarious puny party game for your hands! Use your hands to preform funny and face-paced challenges.

The hilarious game where perception is deception!

It sounds simple - put on the vision-altering Joggles and race to complete a series of tasks with your teammate. Throw the ball to each other, high five someone, write your name…easy-peasy, right?

Not in Joggles! Try to watch the chaos unfold as you’re left wondering which was is which!

Game is played in 4 rounds: 2 team challenges (one team vs the other) and 2 VS challenges (individual players on each team against each other).

After 4 rounds the team with the most points wins!

boardgame: 大洋落日 (Sunset over the Pacific)
description:
大洋落日 (Sunset over Pacific) is an operational, hex-based wargame depicting carrier battles in the Pacific during the dramatic year of 1942, designed by Chinese designer Salm.
In this game, players take the role of fleet commanders, attempting to achieve their objectives and defeating the opponent's forces, while under the condition of limited intelligence from the game’s double-blind mechanics. As an operational game, the key challenges for the players would be to determine how to position their forces before contact with the enemy fleet, and managing the fuel level of their fleet at the same time.

The distinct difference of Sunset over Pacific from other double-blind naval games is that it does not require a referee, yet still allows a full fog of war experience by using the magnetic panels to pass around scouting plans, and the rest of the information is hidden from the other player behind the game screen. Allowing an immersive and engaging experience of Pacific carrier duels between 2 players.

The game includes scenarios for all four major carrier action in the Pacific during 1942: Coral Sea, Midway, Eastern Solomon, and Santa Cruz. For each battle, 3 different scenarios are included:

     The short scenario, where both fleets are already in place, the battle is imminent, allowing the players to jump right into action.
     The long scenario, where both fleets are starting their deployment into the theater, allows the players to experience the historical environment of decision making.
     The historical random scenario, which included a level of uncertainty to the deployments of forces within historical likelihood, to reduce the impact of hindsight from the players and provides more possibilities for the situation.


This adds up to 12 scenarios for the game, and there is also an extra learning scenario for the players to get familiar with the game’s rules and mechanics.

Components:
Three counter sheets with 501 counters (112 of 15mm size, 389 of 13mm size)
Two 446x306mm mounted maps (Midway map)
Two 546x346mm mounted maps (South Pacific map)
Two 1176x210mm operational screens
Four 210x140mm magnetic scouting panels (2 for Midway map and 2 for South Pacific map)
72 magnetic scouting markers
Two fleet charts (210x280mm)
Two pad of planning maps
Two plastic supporter for the operational screens
Two 10 sided dice

Time scale: 12 hours per turn
Map scale: 84 nautical miles (155.5km) per hex side to side
Unit scale: Single ship for capital ships, squadrons for cruisers and destroyers. 18-24 planes per air unit.

boardgame: 南征北战：山东，1947 (Fighting North and South: Shandong, 1947)
description:
南征北战：山东，1947 ("Fighting North and South: Shandong, 1947") is a two-player strategic-level wargame set in the Jiangsu (苏) and Shandong (鲁) theater from late January to late July 1947, during the second Chinese Civil War. It includes three scenarios:
Scenario I: "Battle of Southern Shandong" (鲁南决战) – Covers the battles of Baitabu (白塔埠) and Laiwu (莱芜). The Battle of Laiwu was adapted into the classic 1952 black-and-white film Fighting North and South (南征北战).
Scenario II: "Decisive Battle in Central Shandong" (鲁中会战) – Includes the battles of Tai'an–Mengyin (泰蒙) and Menglianggu (孟良崮).
Scenario III: "July Divided Operations" (七月分兵) – Covers the battles of Nanma (南麻) and Linqu (临朐).

Together, these three scenarios illustrate the shifting strategic balance between the Kuomintang and the Communist forces on the Jiangsu-Shandong battlefield. In August 1947, the main force of the Shanxi-Hebei-Shandong-Henan Field Army (晋冀鲁豫野战军) broke out from southwestern Shandong and advanced into the Dabie Mountains (大别山). Shortly thereafter, Chen Yi (陈毅) and Su Yu (粟裕) led the Western Army of the East China Field Army (华野西兵团) out of Shandong and into the Central Plains(中原), while Xu Shiyou (许世友) and Tan Zhenlin (谭震林) commanded the Eastern Army of the East China Field Army(华野东兵团) as they held their ground in Jiaodong (胶东)—marking a new chapter on the Jiangsu-Shandong battlefield.

The player commanding the Communist forces takes on the role of Chen Yi and Su Yu. Depending on the scenario, the Nationalist player assumes the role of Chen Cheng (陳誠) in Scenario I, Ku Chu-tung (顧祝同) in Scenario II, or Chiang Kai-shek (蔣介石) in Scenario III.

Scale:
1 turn = 3 days
1 hex = 20 kilometers
Most units are at the corps level, with a few at the division or brigade level.

—description from the designer
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Fling your colorful magnetic flaps (yellow, blue, green, red) onto the magic 6x6 Flapboard grid! 
Goal: Build the longest magnetic chain connecting the flaps, steal others and win the game.

How to Play game "CATENELLE" (1min setup, 10-25min):

     50cm x 70cm magnetic board
     28 flaps (7 per color)
     Fling from edges (2m distance)
     Chain = connect adjacent flaps with same or different colour, the last colour attached lead the chain.
     Score: chain length, number of flaps per chain
     Victory: the first player to win 2 sets of 15 points


Modes: there are several game variants from 1 to 10 players, explained in the manual, some of them are:

     Cross Sniper
     Tower Control
     Solo mode


What's Unique: First game with magnetic flaps for real-life flinging – suspense, positioning strategy, infinite replay!

—description from the designer

Years after the great fallout, your community has survived in an underground facility. But now, the power has failed. You must venture into the irradiated wasteland to restore power and destroy the mutant camp.

Nukeland is a semi-cooperative survival game for 1–4 players. Players act as champions for their respective factions, balancing the need to cooperate against mutants with the desire to betray rivals for ultimate glory. The game features area control, resource management, and strategic combat. Players spend Action Points (AP) to move, scout tiles, interact with markets, and upgrade gear. Will you form an alliance to survive, or climb the communication tower to destroy everything in sight?

—description from the designer

In the solar system of Galaterra, three factions (Yaud, Xin, and Arap) live in constant conflict for space supremacy. Players take on the role of Aly, a young pilot expelled from the academy who seeks a second chance to earn a position in the peacekeeping organization CENG. To achieve this, she must complete missions, explore different sectors, and fight space battles.

Mechanically, this gamebook stands out for an "unplugged" system where no dice, pencils, or character sheets are required. Inventory management, ship modules, and sustained damage are tracked using the player's own fingers as physical bookmarks between specific sections of the book. Additionally, maintaining the ship's systems introduces a memory mechanic: if the player forgets which modules are equipped, they stop working.

Battles are resolved through a randomized system integrated into the book's pages. The goal of the game is to survive the adventure, make the right decisions to overcome the main plot, and complete a series of achievements to reach the status of Legend of Galaterra. (Note: The book also includes a classic adventure sheet for those who prefer playing with a pencil and dice).

—description from the designer

CATherapy is a cozy, cooperative, meta-progression game about healing mental health through the comfort of cats. Players work together to manage a cat therapy center, rescuing cats from the streets, building rooms in the office, and matching clients with their ideal therapy cats. Every cat has unique traits, and finding the right matches for each client is central to success.

Gameplay unfolds over in-game days and weeks. On a player’s turn, they may rescue a cat, build a new room, or gather kibble and litter to support their cats. Clients are then assigned to therapy rooms, where the right combination of cats can reduce their anxiety, anger, depression, social struggles, and other mental health symptoms. Poor matches create challenges, including negative reviews and resource loss, adding tension and encouraging teamwork.

Players must carefully manage shared resources like kibble and litter to maintain the office, handle random events, and meet weekly operating costs. Random events create moments of risk and strategy, requiring players to decide when to spend resources or adapt their plans. Each week ends with a cost assessment—if players can pay for cats, room upkeep, and failed clients, they can continue to the next week or pack up the campaign and resume later.

Success is measured by maintaining a thriving CATherapy office while keeping clients and cats happy. As the office grows and more clients arrive, players must balance expansion with sustainability, growing fast enough to meet supervisor expectations while preserving resources. CATherapy combines cooperative puzzle-solving, resource management, and engine-building in a warm, thematic experience that celebrates teamwork, strategy, and the joy of helping others through cats.

—description from the designer

On November 19, 1942, the Soviet counteroffensive "Uranus" was launched.

The Soviets, with seven armies, broke through the Romanian flank of the German 6th Army. The 6th Army at Stalingrad was quickly surrounded, and the tide of battle turned.

Faced with this dire situation, the German general Manstein, one of the most brilliant strategists, launched "Winter Storm," a rescue operation for the 6th Army, on December 12. The German 57th Panzer Corps came within 50 km of Stalingrad.

At this point, the Soviet army launched a major encirclement operation aimed at Rostov. The rescue operation was called off, and the 6th Army's fate was sealed.

In 1943, the focus of the battle shifted from Stalingrad to Rostov.

The Soviet army advanced like a raging torrent. Manstein deployed a mobile defense strategy and successfully defended Rostov Gate.
The 1st Panzer Army retreated safely, and the last vehicles of the 4th Panzer Army, which had been protecting its flank and serving as the rearguard, crossed Rostov Gate on January 31st.
On the same day, the 6th Army surrendered.
Furthermore, around that time, another crisis loomed over the German army. The Soviet army was advancing towards Kharkiv and the Dnieper River. If the Soviet army crossed the Dnieper River, the entire German Southern Army would be surrounded, and a tragedy even greater than Stalingrad would unfold. ...
However, Manstein waited. Even when the Soviet army recaptured Kharkiv and advanced within 60km of the Dnieper River, he did not move.
On February 28th, he issued orders to his troops. "Fight back!"


"Panzer Keil: Battle of Kharkiv" reproduces the "Kharkiv Offensive" from January to March 1943.
This game shares the rules of issue 78 "Panzer Keil: Kursk". Therefore, if you play one game, you can immediately play the other game just by reading through the special rules.

Number of counters: 252 pieces
1 full map

Game Period: January - March 1943
Game Scope: Red Army's 1943 winter counterattack to the Third Battle of Kharkov

Playing time: More than 2 hours
Number of Players: 2

(translated and adapted from publisher's website)

Sole Survivor is an alien-infested spaceship solo survival challenge. It’s a print-and-play and rather lightweight affair: matches are brutal and short, much like life itself.

This is a solo survival card game where the player must explore the scenario, collect three loot tokens, and survive against hostile Xeno creatures.
Each turn the player rolls a die to determine how many actions they can perform, such as moving across the map or playing loot cards. However, rolling a 1 makes noise and immediately triggers the Xeno turn.
During the Xeno phase, enemy behavior is determined by the Xeno movement system. The Xenos move along the shortest path toward the player and attack if they reach them. Different types of Xenos have unique abilities and attack patterns, forcing the player to carefully manage positioning and resources.
Loot cards provide temporary advantages, defensive tools, or special abilities that can break normal rules and help the player survive.
Across three scenarios the difficulty increases as Xenos become more aggressive. In the final scenario, a Boss enters the map and relentlessly hunts the player.
To win, the player must collect all the loot tokens and survive all scenarios without being killed by the Xenos or the Boss.

—description from the designer

Claim the Crown is a fast tactical card duel played with a standard 52-card deck. Players build their own court by playing cards in front of them, each triggering an immediate ability tied to its suit. Clubs disrupt rivals by striking at their court, Diamonds manipulate an opponent’s hand, Spades recover lost resources from the discard pile, and Hearts echo the powers already in play.

Face cards represent royal authority. They can be played to your court to recruit allies or held in reserve to issue Royal Decrees, allowing a player to cancel an opponent’s move at a critical moment.

As cards accumulate, each player’s court develops into a unique engine of abilities and threats. Every play can swing momentum: removing a key card, stealing from the discard pile, or cancelling your opponent's play.

Victory comes from assembling the right combination of in your court. Unite the four suits under noble leadership or dominate the court with a single faction. The first player to achieve one of these conditions claims the crown and wins the game.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Probably Illegal: The World's Most Dangerous Card Game… Probably
description:
Probably Illegal: The World’s Most Dangerous Card Game… Probably is an adult party game where players solve outrageously immoral fictional scenarios using combinations of Skill and Object cards.

Each round a player presents a bizarre criminal or morally questionable situation. The other players must quickly pitch how they would survive, escape, or succeed using the cards in their hand. Plans are judged by the round’s judge based on creativity, effectiveness, and entertainment value.

Players may sabotage each other (with hidden Backstab Cards), bend the logic of their cards, and attempt to outwit the table with the most convincing or ridiculous solutions.

The game emphasizes fast rounds (30 sec stories), improvisation, and chaotic social interaction. The winner is the player who successfully convinces the table they have the best or worst plan.

—description from the publisher

What's shorter than a short story? Exactly, a mini-story! In this game, you'll work together to memorize a series of quirky sentences and then recite them word for word later. But don't worry, creative mnemonics and imaginative mental imagery will help you master this memory challenge with flying colors!

GlobeRunners is a very simple free (and open source) PnP deck-building card game.

Find your signature playstyle navigating through more than 7500 cards, build your deck around six main factions and three support factions and be the first to travel around the world.

In this simple 1v1 card game you have to navigate a world map that changes based on environmental factors like the day-night cycle, temperature, and sudden cataclysms. The theme centers on a diverse cast of factions—including sturdy Dwarves, disruptive Demons, and agile Miaous—all racing to be the first to circumnavigate the globe. Each faction has favored biomes, such as mountains or oceans, where they move more efficiently.

Gameplay revolves around the "Trip Chain," where players strategically plan their route by playing character cards and support effects. You spend mana to place cards into stopovers, which are then resolved sequentially. During resolution, cards grant movement based on their base value, but they can also trigger powerful effects if specific conditions are met, such as the current time of day or the orientation of the character's face in the illustration. Support factions like Mages or Engineers add another layer of strategy by manipulating the environment or placing traps directly onto the map.

Interaction is key, as you can play cards defensively to block your opponent’s progress or use abilities to cancel their effects and force them backward. To win, you must optimize your deck’s synergy between main and support factions to overcome environmental obstacles and outpace your rival. The first player to successfully cross the finish line and "claim the globe" is declared the winner.

⚠️ All the illustrations in this project are AI-generated. While I am aware of the board game community's reservations regarding AI, this game is a one-person passion project. Utilizing AI for the artwork was a necessary compromise to make the massive number of unique cards.

—description from the designer

In La Grieta, you’ll reveal which side you’re on: sweet or bitter? Is it peet-sa or peek-sa? If a movie is bad, do you abandon it or watch it until the end?

Simple: read the card, choose your side by raising ☝ or ✌, and then discuss—peacefully and harmoniously*—what everyone voted. You win if you think like the majority of the group… or, in the “La Grieta Argentina” variant, like the majority of the country.

Perfect for groups that can survive any debate (sort of). Because if there’s one thing that unites us… it’s that there’s always a divide.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Melted: A Messy Ice Cream Game
description:
Build a big, messy, ice cream cone in Melted, a card game of light strategy and sweet sabotage.

Match flavors on the ever-changing cone or freeze out your competition with action cards that change flavors, swap your hands, or wipe the cone clean.

Be the first to empty your hand or get stuck, holding the mess!

How to play:
In Melted players make one big messy ice cream cone made up of three parts: the top, the middle and bottom of the cone.

Players add to the cone with a matching flavor card, or an action card that can change the active flavor, discard the whole cone, or swap everyone’s hands.

Set up:
Shuffle every card in the deck and deal seven cards to each player. Keep your hand hidden from other players!

Place the remaining deck in the center of the table as the draw pile.

The player whose birthday is closest goes first, starting the ice cream cone with a flavor card from their hand.

Gameplay:
On each player’s turn:
Play a flavor card that matches the active flavor into its corresponding position or on the cone. OR
Play an action card and follow its instructions.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Proof of Life: A Fun Game About Death
description:
Proof of Life is a collaborative conversation card game about death, grief, loss, and reflecting on what you love about life.

Official Rules:
The goal of Proof of Life is to play a set number of cards to assemble a circle with other players. The player who places the last answered or correct card that completes the circle wins the game.

Depending on the number of players your circle will need to be completed with the following number of cards:
One player- 8 cards
Two players- 8 cards
Three players- 10 cards
Four players- 12 cards

Set up:

Take a moment to locate anything you’d like to use for the create cards- we suggest a notebook, a journal, or a stack of colorful paper, along with pens, crayons, or markers- anything that best suits you that will enhance and personalize your Proof of Life experience.

You may also choose to set aside the create cards for when you want to play a longer game, either in solo mode or with other players.

2-4 players:

To begin:

Shuffle and deal out 7 cards to each player. The remainder of the deck can stay in the box within reach of all players.

The person whose birthday is closest to the current date begins the game by choosing any card in their hand to play.

Playing a card:

Three types of cards:

Question cards: 
Direct the question card to one other player by reading it aloud and asking the question. You will then place the question card anywhere you’d like as you assemble your circle on the table.

Think cards: 
You may play a think card by choosing any player to read it aloud to, and guessing how they would answer. If you guess correctly you will then place the think card anywhere you’d like as you assemble your circle on the table, if you’re wrong, discard the card into the discard pile without placing it on the table, and draw another new card from the deck.

Create cards: 
When you play a create card with 2-4 players you’ll have up to 5 minutes to follow the prompt on the card by sharing your answer aloud, or by drawing or writing. You will then place the create card anywhere you’d like as you assemble your circle on the table.

Always draw back up to 7 cards in your hand when playing by the official rules. 
 Play continues clockwise.

The player who places the final card, completing the circle of cards wins Proof of Life.

—description from the publisher

In Enjambre, players become part of the "Panal Dorado" ("Golden Honeycomb") hive, facing nature's daily challenges to collect nectar and protect their home from threats like wasps and storms. It's a cooperative game where the goal is to complete five different missions by working as a team to save the hive.
The game's standout feature is its communication system: to simulate real bee behavior, players must forgo words, numbers, and human gestures. Instead, they exclusively use buzzes of varying durations and rhythms to indicate to the rest of the swarm the location of the richest nectar-producing flowers within the "Campo de las mil Flores" ("Field of a Thousand Flowers.")

To win, the team must reach nectar point targets in each mission, which are earned when other members correctly interpret the scout bee's messages.

The game includes adaptations for visual accessibility (colorblind iconography) and difficulty levels ranging from familiar to more complex challenges.

•••

En Enjambre, los jugadores forman parte de la colmena "Panal Dorado", enfrentando los desafíos diarios de la naturaleza para recolectar néctar y proteger su hogar ante amenazas como avispas y tormentas. Es una propuesta cooperativa donde el objetivo es superar cinco misiones distintas trabajando en equipo para salvar la colmena.
El juego destaca su sistema de comunicación: para simular el comportamiento real de las abejas, los jugadores deben dejar de lado las palabras, los números y los gestos humanos. En su lugar, utilizan exclusivamente zumbidos de diferentes duraciones y ritmos para indicar al resto del enjambre la ubicación de las flores más ricas en néctar dentro del "Campo de las Mil Flores".
Para ganar, el equipo debe alcanzar objetivos de puntos de néctar en cada misión, los cuales se obtienen cuando los demás miembros interpretan correctamente los mensajes de la abeja exploradora. 
El juego incluye adaptaciones para accesibilidad visual (iconografía para daltonismo) y niveles de dificultad que van desde lo familiar hasta desafíos más complejos.

Try to be the fastest at spotting the animals or accessories that don’t have a matching pair.

Watch out! The Tent-Toppler is on the loose. Don’t be the last to slap the table—shout its name when it appears.

You’ll need to stay sharp and be the fastest at the table.

—description from the publisher

This is an 18 card solitaire pocket game. Minecarts and Mochas is dungeon-building puzzle game with a cozy coffee shop theme. The goal is to line up patrons at coffee and cider stands to score points.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Datos No Opiniones: Cine pochocloso y taquillero
description:
Lights! Camera! Action!

Datos No Opiniones: Cine is a family game where players can show their love for movies and remember the films that left a mark on their lives.

It includes more than 100 cards filled with facts, anecdotes, and humor about memorable movies and iconic scenes… the ones that made you grab the popcorn.

Each card has a nearly impossible question whose answer is a number. The player who knows the most won’t always win: common sense and intuition are key, because this is a game of approximation.

On your turn:

Read a question.

Without knowing the answer, place it in the line depending on whether you think the number is higher or lower than the cards already there.

Reveal the answer: if you placed it correctly, you’re safe—but if you’re wrong, you lose a life.

Lose two lives and you’re out! The last player remaining wins.

Datos No Opiniones: Cine is part of the Datos No Opiniones game line (Selección, Sexo, Rock, etc.) and can be played on its own or combined with other editions.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Hovercraft in a Minefield: Alligator Rescue
description:
A Solo Game of Press-Your-Luck Tactical Maneuvering

Carefully pilot your hovercraft through swampy minefield where the alligators are defenseless, the mines are magnetic, and your fuel is running out. Every move demands tactical foresight and spatial planning, as throttle and rudder settings shape your future position. Mines may drift, and a single misguided turn can turn a rescue into a catastrophe. Carefully timed repairs will keep you in the fight, but remember you can’t drive and put out fires at the same time. It's a game of calculated risk and tactical navigation in a swamp full of danger.
family: Theme: Wetlands / Swamps / Bogs / Fens / Marshland

description:
Project Titan is a sci-fi strategy card game set in a distant future where powerful factions compete for survival, influence, and control across hostile space.

Players build custom decks from a pool of collectible cards and command ships, deploy units, and manage limited resources while navigating dangerous encounters and rival factions. Strategic planning, tactical combat, and careful resource management determine whether a player expands their influence or falls to the many threats of the galaxy.

Each match unfolds differently as players explore new combinations of cards, abilities, and strategies within the evolving Project Titan universe.

—description from the designer

Be the first to reach the end of the board by correctly answering the questions. Players take turns rolling the dice and moving along the path, answering trivia questions about zoo animals along the way. Answer correctly to advance, or stay put and try again next turn. A fun and educational game for the whole family!

Svarog’s Curse is a competitive, card-driven strategy game inspired by Slavic mythology.

Long ago, the celestial blacksmith Svarog forged the three realms, Prav, Yav, and Nav, and kept them in balance. When he abandoned his divine forge, that harmony shattered. Now the gods compete for the empty throne, each choosing a mortal champion to act in their stead.

Players take on the role of these chosen disciples, traversing a grid of mystical locations in search of power. As they explore, they uncover hidden cards, claim legendary heroes, wield ancient artifacts, and confront dangerous monsters.

Each discovery presents a meaningful choice. Heroes and artifacts grant powerful effects and Might Points, while monsters offer greater rewards at a cost, forcing players to sacrifice their own assets and take on Curse Points. They are used to reduce your opponents’ final scores, adding a direct and strategic way to disrupt rival players.

On their turn, players spend action points to move, scout, reveal, and claim cards, gradually shaping their strategy and influence across the board.

As the world begins to collapse, the final round is triggered. Players total their Might Points and apply the penalties they have inflicted through Curse Points. The player with the highest final score proves themselves worthy, ascends as the champion of the gods, and determines the fate of the three realms.

Discovering Zero is a card-shedding, engine-building game about civilizations competing to be the first to discover the concept of zero.

Starting with hand of cards and some simple Discoveries, Hunting and Gathering, you utilize clever card plays to activate them (on your and other player's turns) and get helpful Tools. Use Tools to play more cards and get more Discoveries with more powerful effects. You play 1 card and draw 2 cards each turn, so you'll need all the help you can get as you race toward that goal of playing all the cards in your hand and discovering zero.

Once you've played the base game, there are four mini-expansions adding iconic elements of the different civilization games, including war, wonders and more.

—description from the publisher

Tic-tac, time is running out. Pay attention to the details and find the zoom of the images!

When the hourglass is flipped, all players race simultaneously to find the images shown on their personal card among the pictures displayed on the shared boards.

Each time a player spots a match, they place one of their markers on it. When the timer runs out, the round ends and the player with the most markers on the boards wins that round. Between rounds, players rearrange the board positions to create a new layout, keeping the challenge fresh.

Zap Zap Zoo is a fast-reaction game where players race their animal pawns across a board to be the first to return to the zoo. Each player receives a colored set containing a pawn, a slapper, and a tiara fitted with animal ears that determine which character they represent.

On each turn, a player flips the top card from a shared deck. Cards display one, two, or three animal characters. All players must quickly check if their current character appears on the revealed card — if it does, they race to slap it first with their slapper.

The fastest correct slap earns the card and advances that player one space on the board. However, slapping incorrectly means moving back one space. Special spaces on the board force players to swap tiaras with opponents, changing everyone's animal identity and creating hilarious confusion. The first player to reach the final space on the board wins the game.

boardgame: Swarm: A Solo/Coop Expansion for Full Spectrum Dominance
description:
SWARM Mode is the product of two years of design and testing, thanks to the amazing community, creating what is in effect a new game system that complements FSD, allowing players to play solo or co-op.

Enemies are not controlled through random actions, or with a flowchart. Players roll a separate set of Activation dice for the Swarm, and through the Collective Cards must pick between different commands for the Unit to receive.

Every choice brings consequence, and the players are at any point trying to win, choosing the lesser evil while trying to achieve the scenario’s goals, and of course surviving.

Each of the six available scenarios provides different levels of difficulty, by varying the amount of ADs the Swarm rolls, and by setting achievements for the players to unlock. This widens the scope of the game, opens up to many new challenges, mixing and matching between forces and Swarm opponents!

Whether you are an avid solo player, or if you just want to have a friendlier game with friends, or you’re just impatient to try out your new warband, this expansion should be right up your alley.

—description from the publisher

Optional gameplay module that introduces personal Quests for your heroes. Completing them unlocks additional narrative elements and alternative gameplay components. Using it may slightly lower the overall difficulty of the campaign.

A Danish party game that helps you get to know your friends. 
All questions are about you and the other players and your thoughts about dilemmas, past history and values of life.

Point is given by how many gets the same answer and how well you know each other.

In Le Chocolat, you'll split the chocolate bar and claim the flavors that taste best!

Mix up the 36 chocolate tiles and arrange them into a grid. On your turn, split the chocolate into two chunks along a straight line. If there is ever a chunk of three or fewer tiles left at the end of a player's turn, they'll claim that chunk.

Continue playing until all chocolate has been claimed. Then, score based on the rules of the scoring card (that changes every play). Did you get the best pieces, while avoiding negative ingredients? Most points wins!

—description from the publisher

After a hard-fought battle, the game is tied, and it’s down to overtime. The first team to score a goal wins immediately!

Unfold an entire soccer pitch from this tiny box as you line up your squad to face down your rival club. Use clever positioning to set up that perfect shot into your opponents goal, but be sure to protect your own goal as well!

—description from the publisher

Your goal each round is to convey a secret word to the round's guesser. You and your team gets to spell three words in order to do that.

Players spells one letter each. The catch? They aren't allowed to discuss what your clue they're trying to spell. The first time you have a perfect clue in mind, but the person next to you says a letter you weren't expecting at all... well, that's Jibber Jabble.

—description from the publisher

In the 21xx era, eight technology mega-corporations control the world’s economic lifelines, and information has become more valuable than gold. One corporation seeks to break the current balance of power and hires you to infiltrate the systems of rival companies to obtain their trade secrets. Time is limited—you must crack their servers as quickly as possible and secure the critical data that could change the world.

The Matrix Empire (矩阵帝国) is a solo puzzle board game centered on resource building. Within a limited number of rounds, players use computing power dice to hack into the servers of rival corporations. The game features eight different variable player powers and three difficulty levels. By allocating computing power efficiently, players first infiltrate subservers to build up resources, then strike at the main servers at the right moment to complete their mission.

—description from the publisher

This game is part of the first edition of the 9 Cards series.
publishers: 九张牌玩具公司(9 Cards Toy Company)

description:
Everyone knows that line goes up. Will you be left with the most valuable stocks after a chaotic day of exchanges? Stonk Market is a real-time trading game where you YOLO into company stocks like Blammo or Chatterfly. At the same time, watch out for the ever-shifting Stonk Exchange, which will dictate the stock that’s worth negative points at the end!

Will the Glitch & Co stock take you to the moon or will you FOMO into a negative company? This game for wrinkly brains only!

—description from the publisher

You awaken from a deep sleep to find yourself in a strange, empty room. The other people around you look just as confused. Suddenly, a sharp male voice echoes through the room’s speakers: “I want to conduct an experiment with you. The rat who finds the key in this laboratory first may leave. The rest will be eliminated forever.” You glance at the tightly locked coded door and rush off toward the other rooms…

Escape Room Game (密室脱困) is an American-style adventure board game for 2–4 players built around a modular map system. Players take on the role of test subjects trapped in a maze-like escape facility filled with traps and mechanisms. By exploring new rooms and searching for useful items, they try to find a way to escape. The dangers do not come only from the environment—other players are also a threat. After all, only one person can escape, and the others will do everything they can to slow you down.

—description from the publisher

This game is part of the first edition of the 9 Cards series.
publishers: 九张牌玩具公司(9 Cards Toy Company)

description:
In Cowboys Acelerados no Espaço, players must be the fastest in Space, being the first to eliminate cards from their hand by spotting the correct pattern among them.

The game develops agility and the recognition of quantities, colors, and game elements. It encourages verbalization, attention, and perception with each card played.

Wild West Mode: you will need all the cards and the game mat.

With the board, you gain an additional version of the game by choosing in which quadrant to play your card, similar to tic-tac-toe.

Players take turns one cowboy at a time, proceeding clockwise.

You Think You Know Christmas is a conversational card game by Ami Baio of Pink Tiger Games, a fun, nostalgic, and joyful way to engage in Christmas holiday magic.

How to play:
Each player is dealt a hand of seven cards, and play starts with the person whose birthday is closest to the current day.

Players take turns choosing a card from their hand, and picking any other player to read it to, guessing how they would answer.

If they guessed correctly, they discard the card. If they were wrong, they discard and draw a new card.

The first player to lose all their cards — and knows everyone they’re playing with best — wins the game!

—description from the publisher

Challenge your knowledge in the Perguntados Especialistas universe! Get ready to take off and show why you are the best. Answer questions and complete challenges across 6 different categories: Brilliant Minds (Science), Arts and Culture, Music Celebrities (Entertainment), Travelers (Geography), Sports Legends, and Civilizations (History). Use wildcards to support your game strategy and collect character cards. Time is running out! Challenge your friends and gather correct answers to stay on track. The player with the most points at the end wins!

Mixtura is a creative word game for two or more players where quick thinking and imagination are put to the test. Each player starts with six illustrated cards — each card showing a picture on one side and its word broken into syllables on the other — along with three power cards, a screen, and a pawn on a shared board.

Each round has two phases. First, players secretly combine syllables from their cards to form at least one new word behind their screens. Then, one at a time, each player presents their word by placing the cards image-side up and naming each picture aloud. The other players flip an hourglass and race to guess the hidden word before time runs out. A correct guess lets the guesser advance spaces equal to the word's syllable count, while an incorrect guess or timeout lets the presenter advance instead. Power cards add strategic twists, and star spaces on the board grant immunity from attacks. After each round, players swap one card for a new draw.

The game ends once at least one player completes a full lap around the board, and the player who has traveled the farthest wins.

Welcome to the golden age of travel.

As travel is booming and airlines are expanding, it is your goal, as an airport, to lure these airlines in with the ultimate goal of securing your services for their home base.

In this tycoon board game to must build your airport strategically and manage events to beat you opponent in building an international mega hub.

This expansion to Airport Empire adds deeper mechanics and removes elements of chance (no dice!)

—description from the publisher

Gira Trilhos is a quick two-player puzzle game where each player tries to build a valid rail path connecting their train to a shared station. Twelve track cards are shuffled and arranged face-down in a 3×4 grid, with a station card placed at the bottom center.

On their turn, a player chooses one of three actions: flip a face-down card at the end of their current path to reveal its track, rotate an already revealed track card along their path to change its direction, or move their train to a different column. Players alternate turns, gradually uncovering and adjusting track segments to form a continuous route. The first player to complete a valid path from their train to the station earns a reward token, after which the grid is reshuffled for a new round. The game ends when a player collects three reward tokens.

As a loyal subject of the powerful Qin Shihuang, the first emperor of China, your mission is to help build one of the wonders of the ancient world: the Great Wall of China.

In O Grande Imperador, each player must use their strategic skills to build sections of the wall and eliminate their cards before the other players. The one who manages to eliminate all their cards first will be remembered as the greatest builder, earning honor and an eternal place in history!

Puentris is a strategy board game about building paths where players place polyominoes to create a route across the board before their opponent.
Plan each piece carefully, block your rival, and find the best route to reach the other side first — or eliminate your opponent.

About the game

PUENTRIS is a competitive two-player strategy board game where players build and reshape the board while trying to cross it with their pawn. Each turn begins with drawing a card that shows a geometric shape. The player must recreate that shape on the board using tiles of their color, connected to the existing structure.

Placing tiles can complete rows or columns, which are immediately removed from the board. When this happens, tile structures collapse toward the players’ starting sides, potentially destroying paths or causing pawns to fall.

Players move their pawn along tiles of their own color, advancing up to two spaces per turn. The goal is to reach the opposite side of the board before your opponent. However, the constantly shifting board means every placement can either build a path to victory or create a fatal collapse.

How to play

Phase 1: Draw the top card from the draw pile; it will show a shape.

Phase 2: Place tokens of your color on the board to complete the shape. If you cannot do so, you lose.

Phase 3: Remove any completed rows and columns, then make the remaining tokens fall toward each side of the board. If your pawn is on a completed row or column, you lose.

Phase 4: Move your pawn up to 2 spaces orthogonally across tokens of your color; your opponent’s tokens block your path. If after moving your pawn reaches the opposite side, the active player wins.

Phase 5: Pass the turn to the other player.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Star Wars: Battle Panel Blast Game
description:
Can you capture the Millennium Falcon? 
Take command of a star destroyer hammer and alternate turns knocking out the Millennium Falcon’s Blast Panels. But be careful. The player that destroys the hyperdrive loses the game.

—description from the publisher

Game where two or more players take it in turns to hit a plastic model of the Millennium Falcon with Imperial Destroyer shaped hammers and release coloured hexagons, the goal being to not be the person who releases the red (centre) one.

On a turn, players spin a spinner (light blue, dark blue, either, or skip a turn) to determine their move, then bash the ship till one or more pieces falls out.

If more than two people are playing, then the loser is eliminated and further rounds are played until one person is left and they win.

—user description

In Lerion, players command diverse tribes of warriors and creatures divided into two main legions: Light and Darkness, each with their own unique powers and abilities. Set on the distant and surprising planet Lerion, players collect tribes inhabiting the planet and build their deck to battle against opponents. Combat takes place on a board where each player can set up their combat strategy. More than a combat card game, Lerion is a journey through a mysterious world, offering each player the chance to discover its history and secrets.

Passa Letra is a deduction game for two to four players where each participant secretly writes a word on their personal letter cards and tries to keep it hidden while uncovering their opponents' words. Each player sets up a word holder with four or five face-down letter cards, with an alphabet tracker visible to the other players.

On their turn, a player rolls the die and moves a shared pawn along the board, which lands on a specific letter. The active player then chooses one opponent and asks whether their hidden word contains that letter. If it does, the opponent must flip the corresponding card face-up, revealing its position. If not, the opponent marks that letter off on their alphabet tracker for all to see. Special spaces on the board let the active player freely choose any letter from a given range. When a player feels confident, they may guess an opponent's full word on their turn — a correct guess eliminates that opponent, but a wrong guess forces the guesser to reveal one of their own letters. The last player with an undiscovered word wins.

A trivia game by Grow in an exclusive version! This edition of Quest features 330 general knowledge questions so players can test their knowledge, competing on equal footing. There are 6 different subjects that challenge your knowledge: World, Arts & Entertainment, Society, Science & Technology, Sports & Leisure, and Variety.

On each turn, the opponents choose a category and read a question to the active player. Before answering, the player must place a bet using one of their numbered chips (1 through 5), reflecting how confident they feel. A correct answer advances the player's pawn by the number of spaces matching their bet, while a wrong answer costs those points and ends the turn. Answering before placing a bet triggers a penalty — the player must randomly discard one of their remaining chips. The space a player lands on determines the category for their next question, and special double spaces allow choosing between two categories. Once all five chips are spent, players reclaim them and continue. The first player to reach the end of the board wins.

boardgame: Rumors of Wars: Armada – Unity Edition
description:
Captain, that’s an Andromedan Dominator!
Steady on! Let the Vudar ship flee. We are the ISC; we will prevail.

There was war, and there was peace.

Rumors of Wars is the sixth book of the Star Fleet Armada series, bringing the combatants of the Star Fleet Universe into the Starmada game. Within these pages, you will find ships, weapons, and equipment from three new empires: the Vudar, the Inter-Stellar Concordium (ISC), and the Andromedans.

The Vudar were one of many Klingon Subject Races. Due to a unique geographic situation (the radiation belt at the edge of the galaxy), the Klingons had allowed the Vudar to operate a few “police” ships for decades. (The Vudar were immune to the radiation.) As the General War dragged on, the Klingons allowed the Vudar to expand their fleet and help fight the Hydrans. Then the General War ended, and the Vudar wanted freedom.

Weeks after the official ceasefire, the neutral ISC sent hundreds of ships into the neutral zones on a self-appointed mission to keep the peace. The ISC felt that the other empires were composed of dangerous lunatics who needed to be disarmed and kept from causing trouble. ISC ships used plasma torpedoes but counted on the plasmatic pulsar device as the core of their firepower and tactics.

Reports of Andromedan ships had been heard for years, even before the General War, but these were few and far between. While the ships were dangerous, such encounters were so infrequent as to not be a major threat. After the General War ended, dozens (then hundreds) of Andromedan ships appeared and began a war of conquest, intended to take over the entire galaxy. Their technology was unknown, including tractor-repulsor beams, power absorbers, and the displacement device.

Rumors of Wars contains many starships, including everything from frigates to mighty battleships for the three new empires. It also fills gaps in the existing empires, including escort cruisers. Some empires lacking battle stations and mobile bases will now have them. There are four types of ground installations. A separate file is included for ease of printing.

This book also has details on updates to Starmada that have been made since this series first began, allowing players to easily integrate their existing ships and empires with the new ones.

This book is an expansion. You must have Klingon Armada Unity or Romulan Armada Unity. Play will be enhanced with the Starmada Unity Rulebook.

—description from the publisher

The Goal: Be the first "operative" to shed all your data packets (cards) to trigger the "Final Firewall" state, and survive one last rotation without taking damage to win.

The Board: Players play cards onto three active docks (Teal, Purple, Orange).

Matching Rules: You play cards by matching the dock's color, playing the exact same number (Echo), or stepping exactly +1 or -1 from the current number (The Ladder).

Special Mechanics & Arsenal

Tripwire (10) & Defuse (1): Reaching a 1 or a 10 clears the dock, sets it aside, and deals +1 Damage to the next player. As you noted previously, reaching 1 (Defuse) lets you keep your turn and start a new stack, while reaching 10 (Tripwire) triggers a "Hard Stop" that immediately ends your turn and forces the next player to draw and skip.

Grey Modifiers (+1 / -1): Can be attached to Grey or Null base cards to mathematically alter their values and bridge gaps. Opposite modifiers (+1 and -1) can be played together to instantly cancel each other out.

Wilds (Volt & Stasis): Volt acts as a 1 or 10, and Stasis resets a dock to 5. Both overload the dock to add +1 Damage to the stack while letting you continue your turn.

Nulls: Super wildcards that open a dock to any color. Crucially, they can be played out of turn during a "System Breach" as a shield to deflect accumulated damage to the next player.

—description from the designer

In Yakimess, we're a group of friends who went out to dinner at our favorite Japanese restaurant, only to discover they only have one plate of Yakimeshi (mexican/japanese fried rice dish) for everyone. So, we'll have to fight over the ingredients using chopsticks to be the player who can eat the most of this delicious dish.

This is a game of dexterity, speed, and strategy where 2 to 6 players compete simultaneously during 5 frantic rounds using chopsticks to pick up dice-shaped ingredients from a bowl and simulate eating Yakimeshi. The goal is to place these ingredients on our personal plates and complete patterns and challenges as efficiently as possible to be the first to finish and score more points than the others by taking the highest score soy sauce token that gives you extra points during the round.

Plus, each round will have a special rule of playing that will add even more mess to the table, like playing with your non-dominant hand, playing standing up, or even with the plate on your hand. So, do you think you're ready to become the Yakimaster?

—description from the publisher

Coalition Chess is a four-player chess variant devised in the early 1920s by Austrian composer Arnold Schoenberg, better known for his contributions to modernist music.

Rule

Four players sit around a 10×10 board: Yellow (bottom), Black (top), Red (left), and Green (right).

Yellow and Black are the greater powers and each has a King, representing the land forces. Red and Green are the lesser powers, representing the air force and navy respectively.

During the first three rounds, each lesser power may form a coalition with either greater power.

The King is the same, but its shape is elongated vertically.
The Machine-gun is an expendable King-and-Pawn compound piece.
The Guard moves as a Pawn.
The Motorcyclist moves as a Bishop.
The Engineer moves as a Knight.
The Artillery moves as a Rook.
The Tank moves as a Queen.
The Submarine moves as a Queen or a Knight.
The Plane makes two successive Knight moves (thus remaining on the same color), and may not return to its starting square.

Yellow has the first move and may freely arrange its pieces within its designated setup area, while the other players begin with fixed initial placements.
Turn order proceeds as Yellow, Black, Red, and Green.If two coalitions are formed, the game proceeds as a contest between alliances; the coalition that eliminates the king of opposing coalition wins.

Coalitions may be formed during the first three rounds. A coalition can be declared by verbal agreement, by giving check to the King of a greater power, or by capturing a piece belonging to the opposing greater power.

If one coalition is declared and the opposing lesser power does not form a coalition within the first three rounds, each player in the declared coalition receives 1 point, while players who did not form a coalition receive no points.

If two coalitions are formed, victory yields 6 points, a draw yields 2 points, and defeat yields 0 points.

During the first three rounds, Black, Red, and Green may, instead of making a move, rearrange their pieces within their designated setup areas.

This version of Six-Player Circular Chess is designed by Hridayeshwar Singh Bhati.

Bhati's version of multiplayer chess is played on a circular board composed of four concentric rings with a large central blank area. Each player begins with the standard starting arrangement, positioned so that two files separate adjacent players. On the first rank, the two isolating squares between players are marked in red and cannot be occupied by any piece.

When a piece's movement path intersects the central circular zone, it may continue from the corresponding point on the opposite side of the board, maintaining its original direction of movement.
Harmegedo is a triangular chess for 2 to 6 players. It contains 2 queens, 9 pawns, and the number of other pieces are same with classical chess.

Trinity Draft is a strategic 3-player card game heavily inspired by the dynamic mechanics and engaging flow of Japanese Trading Card Games (TCGs).

The game is divided into two main phases. First, players engage in a tense drafting phase, passing and selecting cards to carefully construct their own unique decks on the fly. Synergy and strategy are crucial as you build your arsenal.

Once the decks are built, players enter a thrilling 3-way battle. Utilizing the cards they drafted, players must outmaneuver both opponents simultaneously, unleashing powerful combos and tactical plays to claim victory. If you enjoy the deep strategy of TCGs combined with the balanced starting ground of drafting, this game offers a highly competitive and fresh experience.

SIXSEVEN is a fast and addictive dice game where every decision matters.
Each round, players roll two dice and choose what to do with the result: keep a die, reroll, or push their luck to improve their combination. The goal is to score as high as possible… or achieve the coveted SIX SEVEN.
But be careful—taking risks can cost you a strong roll. Ability cards add unexpected twists, keeping tension high in every turn.
Win by scoring the most points… or instantly by rolling a double SIX SEVEN.
Quick, simple, and full of tense decisions, SIXSEVEN always leaves you wanting one more round.

—description from the designer
